    publisher = "ACM"
}


Abstract:
PCB (printed circuit board) substrates are often single-use, leading to material waste in electronics making. We introduce PCB Renewal, a novel technique that “erases” and “reconfigures” PCB traces by selectively depositing conductive epoxy onto outdated areas, transforming isolated paths into conductive planes that support new traces. We present the PCB Renewal workflow, evaluate its electrical performance and mechanical durability, and model its sustainability impact, including material usage, cost, energy consumption, and time savings. We develop a software plug-in that guides epoxy deposition, generates updated PCB profiles, and calculates resource usage. To demonstrate PCB Renewal ’s effectiveness and versatility, we repurpose a single PCB across four design iterations spanning three projects: a camera roller, a WiFi radio, and an ESPboy game console. We also show how an outsourced double-layer PCB can be reconfigured, transforming it from an LED watch to an interactive cat toy. The paper concludes with limitations and future directions.

## 3. New Interaction Paradigm for Complex EDA Software Leveraging GPT
Valid Text Names:
'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 1-2', 'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 2-3', 'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 3-4', 'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 4-6', 'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 6-8', 'han2023newinteractionparadigm pages 8-9'

BibTex:
@article{han2023newinteractionparadigm,
    author = "Han, Boyu and Wang, Xinyu and Wang, Yifan and Yan, Junyu and Tian, Yidong",
    title = "New Interaction Paradigm for Complex EDA Software Leveraging GPT",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2307.14740",
    month = "Jul",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2307.14740",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2307.14740",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
In the rapidly growing field of electronic design automation (EDA), professional software such as KiCad, Cadence , and Altium Designer provide increasingly extensive design functionalities. However, the intricate command structure and high learning curve create a barrier, particularly for novice printed circuit board (PCB) designers. This results in difficulties in selecting appropriate functions or plugins for varying design purposes, compounded by the lack of intuitive learning methods beyond traditional documentation, videos, and online forums. To address this challenge, an artificial intelligence (AI) interaction assist plugin for EDA software named SmartonAl is developed here, also KiCad is taken as the first example. SmartonAI is inspired by the HuggingGPT framework and employs large language models, such as GPT and BERT, to facilitate task planning and execution. On receiving a designer request, SmartonAI conducts a task breakdown and efficiently executes relevant subtasks, such as analysis of help documentation paragraphs and execution of different plugins, along with leveraging the built-in schematic and PCB manipulation functions in both SmartonAl itself and software. Our preliminary results demonstrate that SmartonAI can significantly streamline the PCB design process by simplifying complex commands into intuitive language-based interactions. By harnessing the powerful language capabilities of ChatGPT and the rich design functions of KiCad, the plugin effectively bridges the gap between complex EDA software and user-friendly interaction. Meanwhile, the new paradigm behind SmartonAI can also extend to other complex software systems, illustrating the immense potential of AI-assisted user interfaces in advancing digital interactions across various domains.

    author = "Cossins, Matthew John and Phang, Sendy",
    title = "New Reservoir Computing Kernel Based on Chaotic Chua Circuit and Investigating Application to Post-Quantum Cryptography",
    year = "2406",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2406.12948",

    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2406.12948",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2406.12948",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
The aim of this project was to develop a new Reservoir Computer implementation, based on a chaotic Chua circuit. In addition to suitable classification and regression benchmarks, the Reservoir Computer was applied to Post-Quantum Cryptography, with its suitability for this application investigated and assessed. The cryptographic algorithm utilised was the Learning with Errors problem, for both encryption and decryption. To achieve this, the Chua circuit was characterised, in simulation, and by physical circuit testing. The Reservoir Computer was designed and implemented using the results of the characterisation. As part of this development, noise was considered and mitigated. The benchmarks demonstrate that the Reservoir Computer can achieve current literature benchmarks with low error. However, the results with Learning with Errors suggest that a Chua-based Reservoir Computer is not sufficiently complex to tackle the high non-linearity in Post-Quantum Cryptography. Future work would involve researching the use of different combinations of multiple Chua Reservoir Computers in larger neural network architectures. Such architectures may produce the required high-dimensional behaviour to achieve the Learning with Errors problem. This project is believed to be only the second instance of a Chua-based Reservoir Computer in academia, and it is the first to be applied to challenging real-world tasks such as Post-Quantum Cryptography. It is also original by its investigation of hitherto unexplored parameters, and their impact on performance. It demonstrates a proof-of-concept for a mass-producible, inexpensive, low-power consumption hardware neural network. It also enables the next stages in research to occur, paving the road for using Chua-based Reservoir Computers across various applications.

## 1. The Field Guide to 3D Printing in Microscopy

    url = "https://doi.org/10.20944/preprints202105.0352.v1",
    publisher = "MDPI AG"

Abstract:
The maker movement has reached the optics labs, empowering researchers to actively create and modify microscope designs and imaging accessories. 3D printing has especially had a disruptive impact on the field, as it entails an accessible new approach in fabrication technologies, namely additive manufacturing, making prototyping in the lab available at low cost. Examples of this trend are taking advantage of the easy availability of 3D printing technology. For example, inexpensive microscopes for education have been designed, such as the FlyPi (1). Also, the highly complex robotic microscope OpenFlexure (2) represents a clear desire for the democratisation of this technology. 3D printing facilitates new and powerful approaches to science and promotes collaboration between researchers, as 3D designs are easily shared. This holds the unique possibility to extend the open-access concept from knowledge to technology, allowing researchers from everywhere to use and extend model structures. Here we present a review of additive manufacturing applications in microscopy, guiding the user through this new and exciting technology and providing a starting point to anyone willing to employ this versatile and powerful new tool.

## 2. A fully 3D-printed optical microscope for low-cost histological imaging

Abstract:
We present the manufacture and characterisation of a fully 3D printed, low-cost optical microscope using both a 3D printed chassis and 3D printed illumination and imaging optics. The required commercial components, consisting of a basic camera for image acquisition and light emitting diode controlled by a Raspberry Pi for illumination, are integrated into the 3D printed microscope with the full design shown for ease of replication. Our 3D printed microscope uses a single 3D printed objective lens with a 2.9x magnification and a numerical aperture of 0.07. To benchmark the imaging performance of the system, we used standard test targets and histological specimens, namely, a Giemsa-stained blood smear sample and a thin section of mouse kidney stained with Haemotoxylin and Eosin. We demonstrated that sub-cellular resolution was obtained, and we corroborated this by imaging individual red blood cells and intricate anatomical details of the stained mouse kidney section. All of this was achieved using entirely 3D printed hardware and optics, at a fraction of the cost of a commercial brightfield microscope, while presenting remarkable potential for customisation and increased accessibility for diagnostic imaging applications.

However most microscopes presented to date are quite novel re‐imaginings of the microscope's mechanical design and they are either solely dependent on, or primarily geared towards, camera‐based observations rather than ergonomic direct vision screening through an ocular lens. The reliance on camera, computer and monitor for observation introduces a compromise between portability, cost and the quality of an instant wide field of view.

## 4. An open-sourced 3D printable microscope with a large CNC stage

Abstract:
3D printed laboratory tools are a fast-developing field, and microscopes in particular have seen several high-quality projects reported in recent years. However, currently available projects for 3D printed microscopes have not been optimised for molecular biology applications. We present an open-source, 3D-printable optical microscope that has been specifically designed for affordability, ease of use, and versatility in molecular biology applications, particularly for fluorescence detection, time lapse imaging, and higher throughput scanning of microtitre plates. The microscope integrates a large computer numerically controlled (CNC) stage, allowing precise scanning of wells in standard microtiter plates, and is equipped with a Raspberry Pi HQ camera module and customizable optical components, including standard microscope objectives. The modular design allows for a range of illumination setups, such as a 3 W blue LED for basic epifluorescence, and supports additional modifications, including dark field or RGB matrix lighting. The design opens new possibilities for cheap home-built microscopes for bioscience applications that are modular and easily adaptable for specific user needs.

## 6. Facile assembly of an affordable miniature multicolor fluorescence microscope made of 3D-printed parts enables detection of single cells
Valid Text Names:
'tristanlandin2019facileassemblyof pages 1-2', 'tristanlandin2019facileassemblyof pages 2-4', 'tristanlandin2019facileassemblyof pages 4-6', 'tristanlandin2019facileassemblyof pages 6-8', 'tristanlandin2019facileassemblyof pages 8-10', 'tristanlandin2019facileassemblyof pages 10-12', 'tristanlandin2019facileassemblyof pages 12-14', 'tristanlandin2019facileassemblyof pages 14-16', 'tristanlandin2019facileassemblyof pages 16-17'

Abstract:
Fluorescence microscopy is one of the workhorses of biomedical research and laboratory diagnosis; however, their cost, size, maintenance, and fragility has prevented their adoption in developing countries or low-resource settings. Although significant advances have decreased their size, cost and accessibility, their designs and assembly remain rather complex. Here, inspired on the simple mechanism from a nut and a bolt we report the construction of a portable fluorescence microscope that operates in bright field mode and in three fluorescence channels: UV, green, and red. It is assembled in under 10 min from only six 3D printed parts and basic electronic components that can be readily purchased in most locations or online for US $85. Adapting a microcomputer and a touch LCD screen, the microscope can capture time-lapse images and videos. We characterized its resolution and illumination conditions and benchmarked its performance against a high-end fluorescence microscope by tracking a biological process in single cells. We also demonstrate its application to image cells inside a microfluidic device in bright-field and fluorescence mode. Our microscope fits in a CO2 chamber and can be operated in time-lapse mode. Our portable microscope is ideal in applications where space is at a premium, such as lab-on-a-chips or space missions, and can find applications in clinical research, diagnostics, telemedicine and in educational settings.

Abstract:
A microscope is an essential tool in biosciences and production quality laboratories for unveiling the secrets of microworlds. This paper describes the development of MicroHikari3D, an affordable DIY optical microscopy platform with automated sample positioning, autofocus and several illumination modalities to provide a high-quality flexible microscopy tool for labs with a short budget. This proposed optical microscope design aims to achieve high customization capabilities to allow whole 2D slide imaging and observation of 3D live specimens. The MicroHikari3D motion control system is based on the entry level 3D printer kit Tronxy X1 controlled from a server running in a Raspberry Pi 4. The server provides services to a client mobile app for video/image acquisition, processing, and a high level classification task by applying deep learning models.

Abstract:
Open hardware solutions are increasingly being chosen by researchers as a strategy to improve access to technology for cutting-edge biology research. The use of DIY technology is already widespread, particularly in countries with limited access to science funding, and is catalyzing the development of open-source technologies. Beyond financial accessibility, open hardware can be transformational for the access of laboratories to equipment by reducing dependence on import logistics and enabling direct knowledge transfer. Central drivers to the adoption of appropriate open-source technologies in biology laboratories around the world are open sharing, digital fabrication, local production, the use of standard parts, and detailed documentation. This Essay examines the global spread of open hardware and discusses which kinds of open-source technologies are the most beneficial in scientific environments with economic and infrastructural constraints.

Abstract:
As 3D printing technologies become more accessible, chemists are beginning to design and develop their own bespoke printable devices particularly applied to the field of flow chemistry. Designing functional flow components can often be a lengthy and laborious process requiring complex 3D modelling and multiple design iterations. In this work, we present an easy to follow design workflow for minimising the complexity of this design optimization process. The workflow follows the development of a 3D printable ‘toolkit’ of common fittings and connectors required for constructing basic flow chemistry configurations. The toolkit components consist of male threaded nuts, junction connectors and a Luer adapter. The files have themselves been made freely available and open source. The low cost associated with the toolkit may encourage educators to incorporate flow chemistry practical work into their syllabus such that students may be introduced to the principles of flow chemistry earlier on in their education and furthermore, may develop an early appreciation of the benefits of 3D printing in scientific research. In addition to the printable toolkit, the use of the 3D modelling platform – Rhino3D has been demonstrated for its application in fluidic reactor chip design modification. The simple user interface of the programme reduces the complexity and workload involved in printable fluidic reactor design. Graphical abstract

## 5. 3D printer-controlled syringe pumps for dual, active, regulable and simultaneous dispensing of reagents. Manufacturing of immunochromatographic test strips

    title = "3D printer-controlled syringe pumps for dual, active, regulable and simultaneous dispensing of reagents. Manufacturing of immunochromatographic test strips",

Abstract:
Lateral flow immunoassays (LFIA) are widely used worldwide for the detection of different analytes because they combine multiple advantages such as low production cost, simplicity, and portability, which allows biomarkers detection without requiring infrastructure or highly trained personnel. Here we propose to provide solutions to the manufacturing process of LFIA at laboratory-scale, particularly to the controlled and active dispensing of the reagents in the form the Test Lines (TL) and the Control Lines (CL). To accomplish this task, we adapted a 3D printer to also control Syringe Pumps (SP), since the proposed adaptation of a 3D printer is easy, free and many laboratories already have it in their infrastructure. In turn, the standard function of the 3D printer can be easily restored by disconnecting the SPs and reconnecting the extruder. Additionally, the unified control of the 3D printer enables dual, active, regulable and simultaneous dispensing, four features that are typically found only in certain high-cost commercial equipment. With the proposed setup, the challenge of dispensing simultaneously at least 2 lines (CL and TL) with SPs controlled by a 3D printer was addressed, including regulation in the width of dispensed lines within experimental limits. Also, the construction of a LFIA for the detection of leptospirosis is shown as a practical example of automatized reagent dispensing.

    issue = "7",
    issn = "1932-6203"
}


Abstract:
Autoinjectors have become popular modern injectable medical devices used as drug delivery systems. Due to their ease, capability and reliability compared to other conventional injectable medical devices, the market and manufacturing demand for autoinjector devices are increasing rapidly and expected to reach a market of $37.5 billion globally by 2025. Although autoinjectors can offset healthcare treatment costs through self-administered medication, they can be expensive for consumers, which limit their accessibility. This study describes the design and manufacture of a spring-driven and 3-D printed autoinjector to overcome this economic accessibility challenge. The digitally replicable device is released as open-source hardware to enable low-cost distributed manufacturing. The bill of materials and assembly instructions are detailed, and the effectiveness of the autoinjector is tested against the current standard (ISO 11608–1:2022) for needle-based injection systems. The safety and dosing accuracy was tested by measuring the weight of 100% ethyl alcohol expelled from six BD Insulin syringes with varying capacities or needle lengths. A one-way analysis assessed the variability between the dose delivery efficiency of 1mL, 0.5mL, and 0.3mL syringes. Testing indicated that the entire dose was delivered over 97.5% of the time for 1mL and 0.5mL syringes, but the autoinjector’s loaded spring force and size exceeded structural limitations of 0.3mL or smaller syringes. Components can be manufactured in about twelve hours using an open-source desktop RepRap-class fused filament 3-D printer. The construction requires two compression springs and 3-D printed parts. The total material cost of CAD$6.83 is less than a tenth of comparable commercial autoinjectors, which makes this approach promising. The autoinjector, however, is a class two medical device and must be approved by regulators. Future work is needed to make distributed manufacturing of such medical devices feasible and reliable to support individuals burdened by healthcare costs.

Abstract:
We propose a design for a simple paste extruder modification that can be used for the selective deposition and patterning of gels and pastes, using a desktop 3D printer as the primary platform. This technology has found use with a variety of materials in seemingly disparate fields, including the printing of ceramics, food and biological materials, each with a variety of material-specific solutions to enhance printability. However, we focus on a syringe-pump driven system that is simple, low-cost, modular, easily assembled and highly modifiable with a low barrier of entry in order to maximise the generalisability and range of printable materials.

Abstract:
This study provides designs for a low-cost, easily replicable open-source lab-grade digital scale that can be used as a precision balance. The design is such that it can be manufactured for use in most labs throughout the world with open-source RepRap-class material extrusion-based 3-D printers for the mechanical components and readily available open-source electronics including the Arduino Nano. Several versions of the design were fabricated and tested for precision and accuracy for a range of load cells. The results showed the open-source scale was found to be repeatable within 0.05 g with multiple load cells, with even better precision (0.005 g) depending on load cell range and style. The scale tracks linearly with proprietary lab-grade scales, meeting the performance specified in the load cell data sheets, indicating that it is accurate across the range of the load cell installed. The smallest load cell tested (100 g) offers precision on the order of a commercial digital mass balance. The scale can be produced at significant cost savings compared to scales of comparable range and precision when serial capability is present. The cost savings increase significantly as the range of the scale increases and are particularly well-suited for resource-constrained medical and scientific facilities.

Abstract:
The weight measurement system is carried out manually using a manual scale. The existing weighing system is still considered inefficient because it takes a long time if it is done repeatedly and there are too many errors in its measurement. To overcome this, an electronic weighing device was designed using the NodeMCU ESP 8266 microcontroller as a controller and a load cell as a sensor. This journal presents the development of electronic weighing indicators for digital measurements. The purpose of this system is to read the measured weight in conventional analog form to digital form, achieving high precision in measurement and calibration. The components used in this research are Load Cell, Load Cell Hx711 amplifier, NodeMCU ESP 8266 microcontroller, and LCD module. In this study, a 4 kg load cell was used. The load cell sends the output signal of the measured mechanical weight to the Hx711 module which amplifies and sends the output to the NodeMCU microcontroller. The microcontroller calibrates the output signal with the help of the load cell amplifier module before sending the converted signal to digital form to the LCD module for display. The developed system has proven that digital electronic weighing systems can be low cost, miniature, discrete, and can take accurate readings without errors

Abstract:
<jats:p>ABSTRACT Weighing lysimeters, which directly measure mass variations in the soil-plant system, provide high precision, but are typically costly, limiting their large-scale use. As an affordable alternative, six low-cost weighing lysimeters were developed, calibrated, and evaluated. The devices were built with low-cost materials and a modular design, integrating load cells with an Arduino microcontroller platform (ATmega 2560). The calibration with reference masses demonstrated excellent linearity, with determination coefficients (R2) ranging from 0.9890 to 0.9987, confirming a high measurement accuracy. The error parameters were also rigorously assessed, including hysteresis (up to 6.33 % of the full scale - FS), non-linearity (up to 10.92 % of the FS), and repeatability (up to 14.57 % of the FS). Despite the calibration consistency, the analysis of these errors and the maximum absolute error (up to 10.77 % of the FS) revealed limitations in achieving the precision required for highly sensitive scientific applications.</jats:p>

Abstract:
Speaking weight scale is an important low vision health aid which measures and announces out the measured weight. It is valuable in numerous applications such as Bathroom scale, Kitchen scale and more. Different talking scales have been developed for blind community. Many talking scales have language option for English, German, French or Spanish. However, only limited work exists for Swahili speaking visually impaired community in East African Community (EAC) given the fact that no talking scale can announce weight in Swahili, which is the common language in EAC. Therefore, this project aims to develop a Swahili speaking weighing machine to assist visually impaired people in Tanzania. The proposed speaking scale is divided into two major parts. On the front-end of the design, sensors are used to capture weight parameters. The captured values are mapped onto sequence of voice patterns. The back-end consists of transferring a sequence of voice patterns to a loudspeaker whereby the voice patterns are stored on an SD card. Finally, the developed device has been evaluated on several objects (certified scale calibration weights) with known weights. Each object was reweighed two times. Placed certified calibration weights on the scale and note the output. Took the measured object off the scale and let the scale return to zero. Placed the same object on the scale again. Noted the output again. The results then showed that the scale displayed the same weights on each object. The expected weight of given objects was then compared with the recorded ones to assess the performance of the scale. The results then showed that the scale is able to measure objects, displays digital output of measured weight and announce it in Swahili language within the accuracy of 1% error range of the actual weight. The proposed device has a great potential as a low vision health aid for Swahili speaking. The features of this device can be further improved to increase the autonomy of blind people to use the device and navigate to the device’s location safely.

Abstract:
Highlights What are the main findings? We showed that it is possible to develop a cost-effective and robust SPA measurement system using user-friendly Arduino-based instrumentation. We analyzed SPA data in ways never explored before, adapting mathematical strategies, exploring SPA on a minute-by-minute basis, and classifying it into four distinct domains. What are the implications of the main finding? By offering a measurement method based on accessible instrumentation, we are contributing to the advancement of SPA-related research. Our expectation is that constant SPA monitoring could become a standard practice in both scientific research and veterinary settings. Abstract Background: Weight-based methods can be cost-effective and practical for measuring spontaneous physical activity (SPA) in laboratory animals, but their adoption and exploration of analyses remain limited. Methods: We demonstrate the construction of a balance using accessible components (iron plates and open-source Arduino® electronics) and provide detailed instructions to enable others to build their own systems. Additionally, we propose new analytical strategies, such as using the Mean of Weight Changes (MWC), assessing the dispersion of weight changes, and classifying SPA into domains, to enhance data interpretation. Results: The construction of the weighing balance using accessible components proved to be feasible, and the balance demonstrated sensitivity in distinguishing high SPA under experimental conditions known to modulate it (dark/light phases and small vs. large cages). Regarding the analyses, we were able to confirm that MWC analysis is a valid measure of SPA. Furthermore, the coefficient of variation in weight changes could be used as a complementary analysis to MWC. The proposed SPA domains also proved to be valid, as they aligned with the understanding that rodents spend a greater proportion of time in the higher SPA domains during the dark phase, while lower SPA domains predominate during the light phase. Conclusions: Our findings reinforce the robustness and validity of our weighing balance, designed using a low-cost setup based on iron plates and open-source Arduino® electronics.

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the design principles, electronics architecture, and documentation norms for open-source scientific hardware instruments like OpenFlexure, Poseidon syringe pumps, Jubilee, and the GOSH community? How do they handle PCB design, modular electronics, stepper motor drivers, and design for manufacture?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'booeshaghi2019designprinciplesfor pages 1-3'.

A. Sina Booeshaghi, Eduardo da Veiga Beltrame, Dylan Bannon, Jase Gehring, and Lior Pachter. Design principles for open source bioinstrumentation: the poseidon syringe pump system as an example. bioRxiv, Jan 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1101/521096, doi:10.1101/521096. This article has 7 citations.

This excerpt describes the Poseidon open-source syringe pump and microscope as an example of open bioinstrumentation design. The authors emphasize six high-level design principles: functionality, robustness, simplicity, modularity, benchmarking, and documentation. Poseidon is implemented to be low-cost (<$400) and quick to assemble (under an hour), using off-the-shelf components and 3D-printed parts. Its electronics architecture couples a Raspberry Pi with touchscreen for the microscope and an Arduino board with a CNC shield to operate up to four pumps; each pump uses a stepper motor driving a lead screw and sled to actuate syringe plungers. The project repository (BSD 2-clause license) includes CAD files, GUI pump controller software, and Arduino firmware; the authors provided direct links to the specific parts for reproducibility. The paper frames design for two user types: straightforward adopters and those who will adapt/improve designs, motivating clear documentation and modularity. The excerpt does not provide detailed discussion of custom PCB design, specific stepper-driver chips, modular PCB architectures, or explicit design-for-manufacture guidance for PCBs — those topics are not covered here.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'booeshaghi2019designprinciplesfor pages 3-5'.

A. Sina Booeshaghi, Eduardo da Veiga Beltrame, Dylan Bannon, Jase Gehring, and Lior Pachter. Design principles for open source bioinstrumentation: the poseidon syringe pump system as an example. bioRxiv, Jan 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1101/521096, doi:10.1101/521096. This article has 7 citations.

The Poseidon project emphasizes three core design principles: robustness (tolerating manufacturing and operational variability), simplicity (easy sourcing, assembly, and software installation), and modularity (independent interchangeable units and use of standard parts). Functional requirements drove choices: precise syringe pumps (~1 mL/hr), a capable microscope, multi-pump independent control, and a simple user interface that can change flow rates, syringe size, gradients, and operate the microscope. Electronics architecture centers on an Arduino Uno controlling steppers via a CNC shield and stepper motor drivers, with a Python GUI (built with Qt Designer) communicating over USB to coordinate microscope and pumps. Practical lessons include handling mechanical tolerance for 3D-printed parts, reinforcing structures and securing rods, and avoiding software/hardware mismatches (e.g., microstepping). Documentation and distribution norms favor an accurate BOM with off-the-shelf vendors (Amazon), minimal special tools, instructional videos, simple firmware loading instructions, compiled single-click executables for major OSes, and instructions for Raspberry Pi usage. The excerpt does not discuss PCB layout practices, Jubilee/OpenFlexure/GOSH-specific norms, or detailed design-for-manufacture PCB decisions.


---

## pqac-00000002

From text 'booeshaghi2019principlesofopen pages 2-3'.

A. Sina Booeshaghi, Eduardo da Veiga Beltrame, Dylan Bannon, Jase Gehring, and Lior Pachter. Principles of open source bioinstrumentation applied to the poseidon syringe pump system. Scientific Reports, Aug 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-48815-9, doi:10.1038/s41598-019-48815-9. This article has 80 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt outlines guiding design principles for open-source bioinstruments (Poseidon): functionality, robustness, safety, simplicity, modularity, benchmarking, and documentation. It emphasizes specifying functional requirements up-front (example: syringe pumps capable of ~1 mL/hr for monodisperse emulsions; microscope magnification; independent control of at least three pumps; simple software controls for flow rates, syringe selection, gradients; microscope control). Robustness is stressed both mechanically and in software: design tolerances to accommodate variation in 3D-printed parts (e.g., press-fit syringe, reinforced body, set screws), many design-build-test iterations, and thorough software testing across operating systems. A concrete software/hardware integration issue is given: mismatched microstepping configuration caused incorrect flow rates, illustrating the need for end-to-end testing and usability tweaks. Documentation, modularity, and small usability refinements are framed as critical to adoption. The excerpt, however, does not provide detailed electronics architecture, PCB design practices, modular electronics topologies, stepper motor driver choices, or explicit design-for-manufacture guidance for PCBs — those topics are not covered here.


---

## pqac-00000003

From text 'keller2023developmentandsharing pages 9-13'.

Oliver Keller, Stefan Appelhoff, Benjamin Paffhausen, and Tobias Wenzel. Development and sharing of open science hardware: lessons learned from wikimedia fellowships. Research Ideas and Outcomes, Jan 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.3897/rio.9.e95174, doi:10.3897/rio.9.e95174. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes principles and practical choices used when developing open scientific hardware for microfluidics and behavioral devices: prioritize low-cost, modular, locally-fabricable designs that are easy to understand and modify; document development on public GitHub repositories (e.g., github.com/wenzel-lab/open-microfluidics-workstation) to capture notes, resources and purchased parts. Electronics architectures favor modular controller boards (example: a custom Raspberry Pi hat with separate strobe and pressure/flow controller boards) and use accessible platforms where possible (they selected a RedPitaya board "[that] allows python control with minimal Verilog programming"). However, trade-offs remain: some FPGA toolchains still depend on proprietary tools ("This board still relies on proprietary and rather complicated software (Vivado)") and sourcing "no-name modules" can be difficult, often forcing developers to "buy the separate components and combine them on custom PCBs." Reproducibility and manufacturability are recurring challenges — prototypes ranged from "flying cables" to PCB-based builds with motor reliability or power consumption issues — and reaching a publishable intermediate state can be time-consuming. The authors note that open hardware commonly reuses modular elements (code, schematics, 3D models) from public projects and that wider open-source toolchains (Yosys, f4pga) are emerging to improve accessibility.


---

## pqac-00000004

From text 'booeshaghi2019principlesofopen pages 5-7'.

A. Sina Booeshaghi, Eduardo da Veiga Beltrame, Dylan Bannon, Jase Gehring, and Lior Pachter. Principles of open source bioinstrumentation applied to the poseidon syringe pump system. Scientific Reports, Aug 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-48815-9, doi:10.1038/s41598-019-48815-9. This article has 80 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes key design principles used in the Poseidon open-source syringe pump project: prioritize modularity and use of standardized, common parts to make devices easy to modify, repurpose, and manufacture. Software design emphasizes flexible, user-friendly GUIs (built with Qt Designer), cross-platform single-click executables for Mac/Windows/Linux, and optional Raspberry Pi operation (via Raspbian SD image). The electronics architecture centers on readily available hobbyist platforms: an Arduino Uno + CNC shield for motor control (with accessible microstepping jumpers to change flow-rate behavior), and firmware loaded to the Arduino. The authors stress benchmarking against commercial instruments and transparent reporting of limitations. Documentation norms include thorough build videos, labeled photos, short written instructions, and hosting source and versioned files on GitHub to support collaboration and reproducible assembly. The excerpt documents how these choices (standard parts, modular electronics, simple stepper driver configuration, clear documentation and version control) support adoption and manufacturability. Note: this excerpt focuses on the Poseidon system; it does not provide explicit details on PCB layout practices, Jubilee, OpenFlexure, or GOSH-specific norms.


---

## pqac-00000005

From text 'hohlbein2022openmicroscopyin pages 12-16'.

Johannes Hohlbein, Benedict Diederich, Barbora Marsikova, Emmanuel G. Reynaud, Séamus Holden, Wiebke Jahr, Robert Haase, and Kirti Prakash. Open microscopy in the life sciences: quo vadis? Nature Methods, 19:1020-1025, Aug 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41592-022-01602-3, doi:10.1038/s41592-022-01602-3. This article has 60 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt emphasizes open-hardware best practices relevant to open scientific instruments (e.g., OpenFlexure): request and follow standards to enable long-term inter-device operability, and include calibration control points and sample standards for both hardware and software. It highlights differences between distributing software (easy ready-to-install packages) and hardware (assembly, part sourcing, non-zero duplication cost), and recommends publishing design files both as vectorized/raw source files and as processed exports. For CAD, avoid sharing only STL (surface-only, no scale); prefer open-source CAD formats or cloud links (openSCAD, FreeCAD, Fusion360/Tinkercad) to enable modification. The open source hardware association (OSHWA) guidance is recommended. There is currently no dedicated microscopy-hardware repository, so thorough documentation, community forums/helpdesks (OpenFlexure example), and clear communication on project scope and support expectations are critical. On electronics/software integration, availability of open-source device drivers and adapters is crucial; plugin-based architectures (µManager) enable wide device support and community-contributed adapters. The text advocates merging expertise across projects (re-using stages/modules), strong documentation, active user communities, and early identification of funding/support to sustain long-term maintenance and encourage modular, reusable designs.


---

## pqac-00000006

From text 'booeshaghi2019designprinciplesfor pages 5-7'.

A. Sina Booeshaghi, Eduardo da Veiga Beltrame, Dylan Bannon, Jase Gehring, and Lior Pachter. Design principles for open source bioinstrumentation: the poseidon syringe pump system as an example. bioRxiv, Jan 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1101/521096, doi:10.1101/521096. This article has 7 citations.

The provided excerpt, focused on the Poseidon open-source syringe pump and USB microscope system, emphasizes core design principles relevant to open-source scientific hardware: use of standardized, common parts to simplify modification and manufacture (noting that most Poseidon components are available from open-source desktop 3D printers); modularity and customizability (poseidon is a modular syringe pump array/USB microscope); rigorous benchmarking and transparent comparisons with commercial systems (they benchmarked droplet generation vs a Harvard Apparatus system and report comparable variance); and clear, user-centered documentation practices. Documentation norms highlighted include short labeled photos, build videos (YouTube) showing full assembly, and use of version-controlled repositories (GitHub) to track changes and host guides. On electronics, the excerpt indicates reliance on common 3D-printer/CNC electronics: a CNC shield with microstepping jumpers is used to alter microstepping and thus access different flow-rate ranges, implying use of stepper motor drivers compatible with such shields. The excerpt does not discuss Jubilee, OpenFlexure, the wider GOSH community, PCB layout practices in detail, nor complete modular electronics architectures beyond the CNC-shield/microstepping approach.

From text 'booeshaghi2019principlesofopen pages 1-2'.

A. Sina Booeshaghi, Eduardo da Veiga Beltrame, Dylan Bannon, Jase Gehring, and Lior Pachter. Principles of open source bioinstrumentation applied to the poseidon syringe pump system. Scientific Reports, Aug 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-48815-9, doi:10.1038/s41598-019-48815-9. This article has 80 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This excerpt describes the Poseidon open-source syringe pump and microscope system and outlines guiding design principles for open-source bioinstruments. The authors list explicit principles: functionality, robustness, safety, simplicity, modularity, benchmarking, and documentation. Poseidon’s electronics architecture pairs a Raspberry Pi and touchscreen for the microscope with an Arduino plus a CNC shield to operate up to four pumps; each pump uses a stepper motor driving a lead screw to move the syringe plunger, and the microscope camera and Arduino connect via USB. Development used standard tools (Autodesk Fusion 360 for CAD; Python 3 and PyQT for software) and relied on off-the-shelf components and 3D-printed parts; the full system (microscope + three pumps) can be assembled in under an hour for under $400. The project is published under a BSD 2-clause license and the repository includes CAD files, pump controller/GUI software, and Arduino firmware, with direct links to parts to aid reproducibility. The authors emphasize that poor documentation and restrictive design choices limit adoption, motivating the stated design principles. function.arguments: {"question": "What is the Jubilee open-source multi-tool motion platform and how does it use modular electronics for laboratory automation? What is the Duckbot? How do self-driving labs use open-source hardware like Jubilee and Science-Jubilee for automated experimentation?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000008

From text 'subbaraman2024theduckbota pages 4-5'.

Blair Subbaraman, Orlando de Lange, Sam Ferguson, and Nadya Peek. The duckbot: a system for automated imaging and manipulation of duckweed. PLOS ONE, 19:e0296717, Jan 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0296717, doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0296717. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper describes Duckbot, an automated system for imaging and manipulating duckweed built on the open-source Jubilee multi-tool motion platform. Jubilee is an open-hardware motion/deck platform (sold as a kit) with documented BOMs and assembly instructions; the project is CC BY 4.0 and certified Open Source Hardware, while the authors’ additions are released under an MIT license with code/design files on GitHub and supporting documentation. The authors treat Jubilee as a modular automation base: Table 1 separates "Frame and Electronics" and other system parts, existing Jubilee toolheads (down-facing camera, syringe, deck attachment) are reused, and the team contributes new tool designs — an expanded 50cc syringe tool, a side-facing camera, and a Jubilee-compatible pipette tool for single-channel OT-2 pipettes. Tools were CAD-designed and 3D-printed. The Duckbot work emphasizes a reconfigurable platform for experimentation (not solely growth-rate measurement) and demonstrates how open-source hardware, shared BOMs, CAD, firmware/software, and interchangeable toolheads enable lower-cost, customizable automated experiments suitable for self-driving or automated labs.


---

## pqac-00000009

From text 'subbaraman2024theduckbota pages 5-7'.

Blair Subbaraman, Orlando de Lange, Sam Ferguson, and Nadya Peek. The duckbot: a system for automated imaging and manipulation of duckweed. PLOS ONE, 19:e0296717, Jan 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0296717, doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0296717. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

Jubilee is an open-source, multi-tool motion platform used as the hardware backbone of the Duckbot system for automated imaging and manipulation of duckweed. Its motion control is built on the Duet3 controller with extension modules and a Raspberry Pi 4 single-board computer, exposing USB/serial interfaces and a web G-code interface for machine control. Jubilee supports modular, 3D-printed and laser-cut tools (e.g., pipette tool with a limit switch, syringes, inoculation loops, top-down and side cameras, deck attachments) that physically attach to the motion platform. The team developed science_jubilee, an application-agnostic Python package and Jupyter notebooks that wrap low-level control (G-code/serial), tool-specific primitives (aspirate/dispense, deck navigation, camera utilities), and OpenTrons-compatible labware definitions. Together these hardware and software components provide reusable, extensible building blocks for automated experiments: easy integration of sensors/cameras, programmatic control via Python, community-sharable labware and tutorials, and the ability to script and iterate experiments — capabilities used by self-driving labs to enable automated, closed-loop or high-throughput experimentation.


---

## pqac-00000010

From text 'subbaraman2024theduckbota pages 1-2'.

Blair Subbaraman, Orlando de Lange, Sam Ferguson, and Nadya Peek. The duckbot: a system for automated imaging and manipulation of duckweed. PLOS ONE, 19:e0296717, Jan 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0296717, doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0296717. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes Jubilee as an open-source, tool-changing motion platform that is controlled from computational notebooks (Jupyter), enabling a direct connection between motion hardware and experiment development/execution. The platform is positioned as a generic motion system that supports multiple interchangeable end-effectors and notebook-driven control. The Duckbot is a customized machine built on this approach for duckweed research: it combines a set of plant-biology end-effectors, software modules to flexibly control those tools, and computational notebooks that run experimental workflows. The Duckbot automates duckweed growth assays including plate setup, image collection, and data analysis. The authors emphasize openness and extensibility—providing design files, build instructions, example data, and analysis scripts on GitHub (duckbot and science_jubilee repositories)—and motivate open-source hardware like Jubilee/Science-Jubilee as a way to lower the barrier to lab automation, let communities adapt systems for niche workflows, and integrate physical experiments with upstream and downstream computational tools for self-driving or automated labs.


---

## pqac-00000011

From text 'subbaraman2024theduckbota pages 16-17'.

Blair Subbaraman, Orlando de Lange, Sam Ferguson, and Nadya Peek. The duckbot: a system for automated imaging and manipulation of duckweed. PLOS ONE, 19:e0296717, Jan 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0296717, doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0296717. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt identifies Jubilee as an open-source, extensible multi-tool motion platform originally presented as “Jubilee: An Extensible Machine for Multi-tool Fabrication” and discussed as a case study in distributed manufacturing. Jubilee has a Motion Platform Kit and an OSHWA certification, and community resources (contributors wiki). “Science Jubilee” is a related open-source project with a GitHub repository and documentation for scientific use. “Duckbot: Jubilee for Science” is an implementation of Jubilee aimed at automated imaging and manipulation of duckweed; the Duckbot paper describes a system for automated imaging/manipulation and links to the Duckbot GitHub. The excerpt also cites work on open-source workflow and management software for interrogating duckweed growth conditions. While these references establish that Jubilee/Science-Jubilee provide an extensible, modular open-hardware foundation used to build lab automation systems (like Duckbot) and that community code/docs are available, the excerpt does not provide low-level details about the specific modular electronics (e.g., controllers, motor drivers, wiring) or exact hardware interfaces; those details appear to be available in the linked repositories and documentation.


---

## pqac-00000012

From text 'pelkie2025automationandautonomous pages 94-98'.

B Pelkie. Automation and autonomous experimentation for sol-gel nanomaterial synthesis. Unknown journal, 2025.

The Jubilee platform is an open-source, multi-tool motion system whose core handles XYZ motion, the frame, motion motors, tool-changing mechanism, and control boards. It is designed to accept interchangeable experimentation tools rather than embedding specific capabilities in the motion base. Science-Jubilee is the Python control layer developed to operate Jubilee and its tools. Available tools include low-precision, high-volume peristaltic pumps, an Opentrons OT2 pipette interface, and an integrated Digital Pipette (open-hardware syringe-based) solution. Modular electronics are used: the Duet board controls stepper-driven pipettes on the motion platform, while individual tools use microcontrollers (Arduino/Raspberry Pi) — e.g., the Digital Pipette was reworked to run an HTTP web server on a Raspberry Pi so multiple syringes can be commanded from the Science-Jubilee Tool class. Camera-based characterization uses Raspberry Pi cameras, and an automated sample-loading system enables integration with flow-cell measurement instruments. This modular combination of motion base, swappable tools, embedded microcontrollers, and a unifying Python API supports automated and autonomous experimentation workflows.


---

## pqac-00000013

From text 'pelkie2025automationandautonomous pages 90-94'.

B Pelkie. Automation and autonomous experimentation for sol-gel nanomaterial synthesis. Unknown journal, 2025.

The Jubilee platform is an open-hardware, tool-changing 3D motion system originally developed for digital fabrication and repurposed for laboratory automation. Its core motion stack handles XYZ movements (frame, motors, tool-changing mechanism, and control boards) and is controlled by Duet control boards running RepRap firmware. Tool-changing enables multi-task workflows—e.g., integrating pipetting, spectroscopy, sonication, or other custom modules—so one machine can perform multiple lab operations within a single experiment. Science-Jubilee builds on Jubilee to provide an open-source platform for automating diverse experiments, supplying compatible tools, and a Python interface to program workflows. Contributors have adapted laboratory devices (for example, a sonication horn) into Jubilee-compatible tool formats and have developed applications such as an automated duckweed experimentation platform. The base kit is relatively low-cost (~$1,700) and assembled from off-the-shelf components, but prospective users should expect a substantial time investment to build/commission systems and note the lack of paid support. The project also emphasizes community-driven extensibility and documentation to lower barriers to entry for automated experimentation.


---

## pqac-00000014

From text 'subbaraman2024theduckbota pages 14-16'.

Blair Subbaraman, Orlando de Lange, Sam Ferguson, and Nadya Peek. The duckbot: a system for automated imaging and manipulation of duckweed. PLOS ONE, 19:e0296717, Jan 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0296717, doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0296717. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes Jubilee as an open-platform, tool-changing automation system for laboratory research that supports integration of new tools and software to enable novel experiments. Jubilee’s design emphasizes interchangeable tools (a tool-changing system) and programmatic machine control, enabling workflows like higher-throughput assays using 96-well plates with a pipette tool and multiple decks. The Duckbot is presented as an application of this approach: a system for automated imaging and manipulation of duckweed that combines interchangeable tools and associated control software to reproduce expected growth-assay results. The authors provide design files, software, and documentation for community reuse and extension. Imaging (top and side cameras) plus frond transfer techniques are used to build image-processing pipelines (e.g., for root-length measurement). The text stresses that integrating existing software is straightforward given their programmatic control approach, and that the platform and Duckbot can be extended to other plants or microorganisms. Community sharing, documentation, and modular tooling are highlighted as key enablers for reproducible, domain-specific automated experiments.

The excerpt describes Jubilee as an open-hardware, open-source, modular multi-tool motion platform (University of Washington) with a modular tool-changing design that accepts user-created tools and beds. It was originally intended for multi-tool fabrication (multi-head 3D printing, multi-pen plotting) and simple liquid handling via syringes. Jubilee can be built from readily available and 3D-printed parts or bought as a kit for roughly ≤∼2,000 USD, and the project provides build instructions and an active community (Discord) to lower the barrier to use. The platform has been demonstrated in automated lab workflows: Politi et al. used a Jubilee-configured setup for automated ultrasound experiments integrated with an Opentrons OT-2 liquid-handling robot and a well-plate spectrometer to synthesize CdSe nanocrystals, testing 625 conditions in triplicate over <2 months. The excerpt does not provide specific details on the term “Duckbot” or on the internal modular electronics design; it does show Jubilee’s interoperability with other instruments (e.g., OT-2) suggesting modular integration but without electronics-level specifics.

## pqac-00000016

From text 'fischer2511modularmultiaxisstepper pages 13-15'.

Mathias S. Fischer, David Grass, and Martin C. Fischer. Modular multi-axis stepper motor driver with remote control for use in microscopy. ArXiv, Nov 2511. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456. This article has 0 citations.

The design uses single-channel Trinamic (TMC) stepper driver ICs (evaluation boards) that "support the SPI interface" and provide incremental encoder input. To simplify construction the authors used TMC evaluation boards rather than bespoke PCBs or chip-scale soldering; wiring is done by soldering to the TMC connectors and using Dupont headers and a small protoboard to break out Raspberry Pi Pico 2 pins, avoiding a custom PCB. Two Pico microcontrollers are used (one in the motor controller, one in the remote); software is written in C++ using the Arduino Pico Framework. Communications shown in the text include SPI (between Pico and TMC ICs), USB serial to the control computer (ASCII G/S commands), and an RJ-45 (8p8c) link for the remote controller to motor controller; all boards are connectorized and stages connect via 9-pin sub-D connectors. The motor control architecture is modular (up to four channels in the described enclosure), with the Pico running tasks: serial command handling, TMC updates, position reporting to remote, and a motor update task that manages homing, pull-in, open-loop and closed-loop moves and checks TMC error/status. The excerpt does not discuss I2C or CAN, nor any HardOps approach by Stirling et al.

## pqac-00000017

From text 'fischer2511modularmultiaxisstepper pages 4-6'.

Mathias S. Fischer, David Grass, and Martin C. Fischer. Modular multi-axis stepper motor driver with remote control for use in microscopy. ArXiv, Nov 2511. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456. This article has 0 citations.

The controller uses four off‑the‑shelf TMC stepper controller boards mounted in an aluminium enclosure, each connected to a stepper motor and optionally to incremental encoders and limit switches via 9‑pin sub‑D connectors. A Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040) is the system microcontroller. The Pico supplies 5 V and 3.3 V to the TMC boards and peripherals and communicates with the TMCs over SPI, with shared data lines and individual chip‑select lines; because TMCs return status bytes, only one TMC is addressed at a time. The Pico communicates to the host computer via USB serial (115200 baud) and to the optional remote controller over two digital lines using UART; the remote plugs in via an RJ45 jack and provides joystick and rotary encoder inputs. Motor control is based on the TMC drivers and microstepping (0–256 MS/FS). The firmware supports open‑loop stepping, “open‑loop with pull‑ins” (re‑commanding based on encoder deviation until within tolerance), and continuous closed‑loop pull‑ins. Some TMC features (e.g., StallGuard) can be enabled. Calibration parameters are stored in flash and can be adjusted at runtime. The excerpt does not provide PCB layout/Gerber details or mention use of I2C or CAN, nor does it discuss Stirling et al.'s HardOps approach for OpenFlexure reproducibility.

## pqac-00000018

From text 'fischer2511modularmultiaxisstepper pages 1-4'.

Mathias S. Fischer, David Grass, and Martin C. Fischer. Modular multi-axis stepper motor driver with remote control for use in microscopy. ArXiv, Nov 2511. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456. This article has 0 citations.

The controller uses Analog Devices/Trinamic TMC5240 driver boards as the low-level motor drivers and a Raspberry Pi Pico 2 microcontroller as the system microcontroller. Low-level current/step control and basic monitoring (encoders, limit switches, basic stall protection) are handled by the TMC boards, which operate inherently in open-loop. Multi-axis coordination, optional closed-loop control (when encoders are present), communication with a host PC, and extra functionality (safety limits, homing, stall detection) are implemented in the Pico microcontroller. The assembled unit mounts up to four TMC boards in an aluminum enclosure, powered by an external supply, with stepper, encoder, and limit-switch wiring routed via sub-D connectors. A separate remote controller (its own microcontroller) provides joystick-based velocity control, per-axis rotary dials for fine stepping, sensitivity adjustment, and a position display. Software support includes C and Python libraries and a µManager driver; design files are hosted at the project's GitHub. The excerpt does not give low-level PCB-layout details (beyond the repository link) nor explicitly state which digital bus (I2C, SPI, CAN, UART, etc.) is used between the Pico and the TMC boards, nor full PCB schematics in the quoted pages. The HardOps approach by Stirling et al. for OpenFlexure is not mentioned in these pages.

## pqac-00000019

From text 'fischer2511modularmultiaxisstepper pages 15-18'.

Mathias S. Fischer, David Grass, and Martin C. Fischer. Modular multi-axis stepper motor driver with remote control for use in microscopy. ArXiv, Nov 2511. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes parts of the electronics and software stack used in Fischer et al.'s modular multi-axis stepper driver. The remote controller is implemented on a Raspberry Pi Pico: encoder handling uses "four state machines in a Programmable IO (PIO) block of the Pico"; the joystick uses two ADC channels (0–3.3 V), a center dead zone, and a continuously running moving-average filter to stabilize readings; button presses are handled by interrupt service routines. The remote Pico loops through tasks to check/send position and command updates and to handle input modes. The motor side "incorporates commercial TMC stepper controller ICs." Host communication uses an ASCII command scheme that the Python and C libraries wrap into friendlier calls (e.g. “set_motor_parameter(2, "CurrHold", 10)” vs “SMP_CHOL2,10”) and the libraries use the VISA standard (pyVISA / NI‑VISA) for PC communication. GUIs (Python tkinter and a C CVI GUI) and an online hardware repository (https://github.com/MCFLab/PicoStageDriver) are provided. The excerpt does not provide explicit PCB design files or layout details, nor does it state use of I2C, SPI, or CAN for inter-board communication, nor detailed motor-control architecture (e.g., microstepping configuration, closed‑loop topology). Also, the excerpt contains no information about Stirling et al.'s HardOps approach for OpenFlexure reproducibility.

The paper describes a motor driver architecture built around one Analog Devices/Trinamic TMC stepper-control IC per axis (single-channel evaluation-board form chosen). The selected TMC supports an SPI interface and incremental encoder input; manufacturer register mappings are exposed via a hardware-abstraction layer so software can be adapted to other chips. The system uses two microcontrollers (one in the motor controller, one in the remote control). Motor-control modes discussed include open-loop, open-loop with pull-ins, and closed-loop; control uses a simple relay-with-tolerance feedback scheme (not heavily optimized). A software flag can reset the TMC motor position to the encoder position after each move to reduce pull-ins. Hardware specifics given include stage motor and encoder counts (200 cts/rev motors on 2 mm pitch spindle, 2000 cts/rev encoders; Z: 4000 cts/rev encoder), and microstepping settings (16x for X/Y, 32x for Z giving 0.625 µm/MS and 0.03125 µm/MS). Missing or not stated in the excerpt: PCB layout and fabrication details, connector/power-stage specifics, whether I2C or CAN are used for inter-board communication, exact inter-microcontroller protocol, and full low-level motor-driver wiring/topology. The excerpt contains no information about Stirling et al.'s HardOps approach for OpenFlexure reproducibility.

## pqac-00000021

From text 'fischer2511modularmultiaxisstepper pages 18-20'.

Mathias S. Fischer, David Grass, and Martin C. Fischer. Modular multi-axis stepper motor driver with remote control for use in microscopy. ArXiv, Nov 2511. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes the electronics approach for Fischer et al.'s modular multi-axis stepper driver: the system uses a Raspberry Pi Pico-based microcontroller protoboard paired with Trinamic TMC evaluation boards (and references TMC ICs/TMC-API), with an available hardware archive at https://github.com/MCFLab/PicoStageDriver. Encoders (either in-motor or on-stage) provide position feedback used for open- and closed-loop operation. The prototype is a 4-channel controller with remote and a reported parts cost of roughly $360 (Oct 2025). A practical assembly issue is extensive hand-soldering between the remote Pico protoboard, input devices (joystick, potentiometer, rotary encoders), the microcontroller protoboard, the TMC evaluation boards, and motor connectors. The authors propose simplifying assembly by designing two interface PCBs (a microcontroller socket plus connectors) and suggest integrating the microcontroller and multiple TMC ICs onto a single PCB to reduce cost—though some TMC package variants have high pin density or no-lead designs that may require professional PCB fabrication. Software components mentioned include a C library using cJSON for config files and integration with µManager and a custom C acquisition package (DukeScan). The excerpt does not specify explicit communication protocols (I2C, SPI, CAN) or low-level motor-control architecture details (step/dir vs. SPI/UART command interfaces), only that TMC evaluation boards / ICs are used and TMC-related APIs are referenced.

       INTRODUCTION


              Open Science Hardware (OScH) refers to any piece of hardware used for scientific
                    investigations that can be obtained, assembled, used, studied, modified, shared, and
                   sold by anyone. It includes standard lab equipment as well as auxiliary materials,
                such as sensors, biological reagents, analog and digital electronic components. Given
                    that proprietary “black box” instrumentation cannot be fully inspected or customized,
               and can be unreasonably difficult and expensive to obtain and maintain, we believe
                   that scientific hardware design should be open to allow for the exercise of these basic
                 freedoms.

               The Global Open Science Hardware community (GOSH) aims at making OScH
                   ubiquitous by 2025. The ability to access, use, replicate, study, and improve scientific
                   instrumentation, which is a central part of experimental science and
                  the public pursuit of knowledge, will contribute to making the concept

    To accomplish independent functionality, components were speciﬁed to ensure that the entire scale
can be powered by a 5V USB power supply, either from a computer USB port or cell phone charging
block, both of which are commonly available at low cost. The components’ power requirements are small
enough that the low-current digital pins on an Arduino can power them, allowing independent control
of power to each component, which is leveraged in the implementation of power-saving features [62–64].
The scale’s tare and calibration functions are controlled with a single push-button—pressed for tare,
and held for several seconds to calibrate.
    As a logging scale, the microcontroller is conﬁgured to interface serially with a computer. The
API for serial communication is built to comply with the Scale Manufacturers Association (SMA)
SCP-0499 Level #2 standard for limited-feature digital scale serial communication protocol. This
includes capability to serially query the scale output, command tare, calibration, and provide basic
scale information [65]. The API allows for continuous tracking of the scale readout for data-logging,
which is useful for automated data-collection in cases such as long-term mass-tracking and discrete
automated processes such as melt ﬂow indexing (MFI).

Article
Open-Source Digitally Replicable Lab-Grade Scales

Benjamin R. Hubbard 1  and Joshua M. Pearce 2,3,4,*

  1  Department of Mechanical Engineering–Engineering Mechanics, Michigan Technological University,
    Houghton, MI 49931, USA; brhubbar@mtu.edu
  2  Department of Electrical & Computerﬁgure Engineering, Michigan Technological University,
    Houghton, MI 49931, USA
  3  Department of Material Science & Engineering, Michigan Technological University,
    Houghton, MI 49931, USA
  4  Department of Electronics and Nanoengineering, School of Electrical Engineering, Aalto University,
     FI-00076 Espoo, Finland
 *   Correspondence: pearce@mtu.edu; Tel.: +1-906-487-1466


 Received: 26 April 2020; Accepted: 25 June 2020; Published: 27 June 2020

 Abstract: This study provides designs for a low-cost, easily replicable open-source lab-grade digital
 scale that can be used as a precision balance. The design is such that it can be manufactured for use in
 most labs throughout the world with open-source RepRap-class material extrusion-based 3-D printers
 for the mechanical components and readily available open-source electronics including the Arduino
 Nano. Several versions of the design were fabricated and tested for precision and accuracy for a range
 of load cells. The results showed the open-source scale was found to be repeatable within 0.05 g with
 multiple load cells, with even better precision (0.005 g) depending on load cell range and style. The
 scale tracks linearly with proprietary lab-grade scales, meeting the performance speciﬁed in the load
  cell data sheets, indicating that it is accurate across the range of the load cell installed. The smallest
 load cell tested (100 g) oﬀers precision on the order of a commercial digital mass balance. The scale
 can be produced at signiﬁcant cost savings compared to scales of comparable range and precision
 when serial capability is present. The cost savings increase signiﬁcantly as the range of the scale
 increases and are particularly well-suited for resource-constrained medical and scientiﬁc facilities.


 Keywords: 3-D printing; additive manufacturing; distributed manufacturing; laboratory equipment;
 open hardware; open source; open-source hardware; scale; balance; mass




1. Introduction

    The incredible success of free and open-source development of software [1,2] is being rapidly
adopted by the hardware community [3,4] as it enables scientiﬁc equipment designers to rapidly build
upon one another’s work [5–7]. This has resulted in a democratization of design building on open-source
designs [8–10] often with open-source tools that enable a true digital distributed manufacturing [11–14].
The most robust examples, is the self-replicating rapid prototype (RepRap) fused ﬁlament fabrication
(FFF)-based 3-D printer [15–17] that has brought the cost of both rapid prototyping and additive
manufacturing low enough to be used by the masses [18–21]. FFF-based 3-D printers derived from
RepRaps now have 3-D printed parts with suﬃcient mechanical strength [22] to be used for ﬁnal
functional parts even if printed on machines that only cost a few hundred dollars. This has resulted in
an explosion of open-source digitally fabricated instruments and a repository of designs housed at
the NIH [5,6,23,24]. There are many examples of progressively more sophisticated open-source 3-D
printed parts being used to build chemical mixing systems [22–25], mechanical components for optics
setups [26–31] and microscopes [32–34], instruments to test water quality testing [35–38], various types
of syringe pumps [39–43] that are combined with other components to make complete systems for


Instruments 2020, 4, 18; doi:10.3390/instruments4030018                              www.mdpi.com/journal/instrumentsInstruments 2020, 4, 18                                                                                      2 of 26



making microﬂuidics and metaﬂuidics [44–47]. Although the most important features of open-source
3-D printable instruments is the ease with which scientists can customize a tool, in general, they are
also much less expensive than equivalent (and often technically inferior) commercial proprietary
systems [6,23,24,48–50] and provide a high return on investment [51,52]. This advantage expands as
the device is made with a higher percentage of digitally replicable components [53]. To continue this
tradition of standing on the shoulders of open hardware giants [54], this paper describes the design of
an open-source largely 3-D printed digital scale. A digital scale is a scientiﬁc instrument that provides
fast measurements of mass generally with an accuracy range of 0.1 g to 0.01 g and have easy-to-read
automatic liquid crystal displays. (Accuracy refers to closeness of the measurements to a known value
of the mass. On the other hand, precision refers to the closeness of the measurements to one another,
which is commonly stated as a single standard deviation of several tests and is independent of the
accuracy (i.e., statistical dispersion).) Digital scales are used in a wide range of scientiﬁc applications
including chemical research, genomics, drug discovery, and proteomics.  Scales are classiﬁed by
precision; where a precision balance has a measurement resolution of 0.001 g, an analytical balance has
one of 0.0001 g and a micro balance has one of 0.0000001 g. On the high-end of scales, an open-source
quartz crystal microbalance (OpenQCM) [55] uses an open-source Arduino Nano and is already well
established in the scientiﬁc literature [56–60].
     Thus, this study provides the designs for a low-cost, easily replicable, open-source lab-grade digital
scale that can be used as a precision balance depending on configuration. The design is such that it can
be manufactured for use in most labs throughout the world with open-source RepRap-class material
extrusion-based 3-D printers for the mechanical components and readily available open-source electronics
including the Arduino Nano. In addition, a validation procedure for quantifying the accuracy and
precision is provided. Several versions of the design were fabricated and tested for a range of load cells,
and the results are discussed in the context of resource-constrained medical and scientific facilities.

2. Materials and Methods

2.1. Design

    This series of open-source scales is based on load cells, which are devices designed to measure
weight or force. The load cells used in this study are strain gage load cells, which convert strain (i.e.,
change in length) of a material into an electrical signal proportional to the force applied [61]. This
series of scales was designed to be easily manufacturable, require minimal components, and oﬀer
the functionality necessary for a basic digital scale, a precision balance, and an analytical balance.
Component location is accomplished entirely by features on the 3-D printed components including
bosses and snap-ﬁt joints, meaning that the only fasteners required are those that hold the load cell in
place. The assembly was designed to fully enclose all electronics, limiting airﬂow, which has been
observed to aﬀect the output of the load cell ampliﬁer.
    At the time of writing, the scale is designed to accommodate two sizes of single-point parallel
beam load cells—TAL220 and TAL221—but is set up to enable fast adaptation to other models. These
were selected ﬁrst due to their low cost, wide selection of weight ranges, and insensitivity to moment
loading on the bed, which aids in measurement repeatability [61]. The base and bed were designed to
be easily modiﬁed to accommodate other load cell styles as required.
    The open-source scale systems were designed for use in two settings: (1) as an independent digital
scale with a displayed output which can be tared (the reference for a reading of zero can be oﬀset
to ignore a known mass, such as that of a container) and calibrated (the output can be corrected to
present the true mass by loading the scale with a known mass) without use of a computer, and (2) as a
serially connected logging instrument for a laboratory setting and data-logging. The serial capability
also allows this scale to be constructed without an LCD, oﬀering a signiﬁcant cost reduction for uses
that do not require a display, such as automated weighing or cases where a computer will always be
used as the power supply.Instruments 2020, 4, 18                                                                                      3 of 26



    To accomplish independent functionality, components were speciﬁed to ensure that the entire scale
can be powered by a 5V USB power supply, either from a computer USB port or cell phone charging
block, both of which are commonly available at low cost. The components’ power requirements are small
enough that the low-current digital pins on an Arduino can power them, allowing independent control

The Guver et al. SECM project emphasizes open-source electronics and low-cost design: a DStat potentiostat (selected for 600 fA / 0.6 pA low-current capability) was used and built "based on an open source project" with a Python GUI. Motion was implemented with stepper motors driven from an Arduino controller, coupled mechanically via a manual XY micromanipulator stage and 3D-printed couplers/gears; motion control used "open source motor control software" (keywords include GRBL). The authors contrast cheap Arduino-based potentiostats (limited to high-microampere ranges and lacking SWV) with the DStat’s low-current performance, noting the full custom-built hardware cost was under $300. The excerpt documents the choice of modular, open-source components (Arduino, DStat, GRBL) and use of 3D-printed mechanical parts, but it does not provide specific PCB modularization strategies, explicit motor driver IC choices, nor detailed design-for-manufacture (DFM) practices. Where relevant, the paper points readers to existing open-source instructions (for DStat) rather than describing custom PCB layout or manufacturing workflows in this excerpt.

## pqac-00000041

From text 'booeshaghi2019designprinciplesfor pages 5-7'.

A. Sina Booeshaghi, Eduardo da Veiga Beltrame, Dylan Bannon, Jase Gehring, and Lior Pachter. Design principles for open source bioinstrumentation: the poseidon syringe pump system as an example. bioRxiv, Jan 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1101/521096, doi:10.1101/521096. This article has 7 citations.

The excerpt describes design principles from the poseidon open-source syringe pump and USB microscope system that are directly relevant to electronics and manufacturability. The authors emphasize standardization and use of widely available components: “Almost all of the components used in the poseidon system can be found in open source desktop 3D printers,” which supports design-for-manufacture and easy sourcing. For motor control the design uses a CNC shield with configurable microstepping: “By physically adding or removing the microstepping jumpers on the CNC shield board users can access a range of flow rates,” indicating reliance on common motor-driver hardware and simple hardware-configurable microstepping rather than bespoke driver PCBs. Modularity is highlighted (“modular, highly customizable syringe pump array”), and the project uses version control (GitHub) for collaborative development and documentation of PCB/firmware changes. The authors stress thorough user documentation (videos, photos, labeled instructions) to reduce assembly errors and speed adoption. They also recommend benchmarking against commercial systems and clearly documenting limitations. These points imply design practices: reuse common, standardized parts and connectors, prefer off-the-shelf driver boards for motor control with user-accessible jumpers, keep the system modular, and provide strong version-controlled documentation to aid manufacture and modification.
