Mathias S. Fischer, David Grass, and Martin C. Fischer. Modular multi-axis stepper motor driver with remote control for use in microscopy. ArXiv, Nov 2511. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes the electronics approach for Fischer et al.'s modular multi-axis stepper driver: the system uses a Raspberry Pi Pico-based microcontroller protoboard paired with Trinamic TMC evaluation boards (and references TMC ICs/TMC-API), with an available hardware archive at https://github.com/MCFLab/PicoStageDriver. Encoders (either in-motor or on-stage) provide position feedback used for open- and closed-loop operation. The prototype is a 4-channel controller with remote and a reported parts cost of roughly $360 (Oct 2025). A practical assembly issue is extensive hand-soldering between the remote Pico protoboard, input devices (joystick, potentiometer, rotary encoders), the microcontroller protoboard, the TMC evaluation boards, and motor connectors. The authors propose simplifying assembly by designing two interface PCBs (a microcontroller socket plus connectors) and suggest integrating the microcontroller and multiple TMC ICs onto a single PCB to reduce cost—though some TMC package variants have high pin density or no-lead designs that may require professional PCB fabrication. Software components mentioned include a C library using cJSON for config files and integration with µManager and a custom C acquisition package (DukeScan). The excerpt does not specify explicit communication protocols (I2C, SPI, CAN) or low-level motor-control architecture details (step/dir vs. SPI/UART command interfaces), only that TMC evaluation boards / ICs are used and TMC-related APIs are referenced.

       INTRODUCTION


              Open Science Hardware (OScH) refers to any piece of hardware used for scientific
                    investigations that can be obtained, assembled, used, studied, modified, shared, and
                   sold by anyone. It includes standard lab equipment as well as auxiliary materials,
                such as sensors, biological reagents, analog and digital electronic components. Given
                    that proprietary “black box” instrumentation cannot be fully inspected or customized,
               and can be unreasonably difficult and expensive to obtain and maintain, we believe
                   that scientific hardware design should be open to allow for the exercise of these basic
                 freedoms.

               The Global Open Science Hardware community (GOSH) aims at making OScH
                   ubiquitous by 2025. The ability to access, use, replicate, study, and improve scientific
                   instrumentation, which is a central part of experimental science and
                  the public pursuit of knowledge, will contribute to making the concept
                and practices of Open Science synonymous with science in the near  OPEN SCIENCE
                     future. Besides having common goals, the community is united by                                           HARDWARE
                 shared values, which were initially laid out in the GOSH Manifesto2
                 but are constantly revisited as the community grows.                  The definition of Open

## 9. hubbard2020opensourcedigitallyreplicable pages 3-7 (Context ID: pqac-00000030)

ucted without an LCD, oﬀering a signiﬁcant cost reduction for uses
that do not require a display, such as automated weighing or cases where a computer will always be
used as the power supply.Instruments 2020, 4, 18                                                                                      3 of 26



    To accomplish independent functionality, components were speciﬁed to ensure that the entire scale
can be powered by a 5V USB power supply, either from a computer USB port or cell phone charging
block, both of which are commonly available at low cost. The components’ power requirements are small
enough that the low-current digital pins on an Arduino can power them, allowing independent control
of power to each component, which is leveraged in the implementation of power-saving features [62–64].
The scale’s tare and calibration functions are controlled with a single push-button—pressed for tare,
and held for several seconds to calibrate.
    As a logging scale, the microcontroller is conﬁgured to interface serially with a computer. The
API for serial communication is built to comply with the Scale Manufacturers Association (SMA)
SCP-0499 Level #2 standard for limited-feature digital scale serial communication protocol. This
includes capability to serially query the scale output, command tare, calibration, and provide basic
scale information [65]. The API allows for continuous tracking of the scale readout for data-logging,
which is useful for automated data-collection in cases such as long-term mass-tracking and discrete
automated processes such as melt ﬂow indexing (MFI).

    The 3-D printable components shown in Table 1 are available to be freely downloaded on the
Open Science Framework (OSF) [81] and are released under a GNU General Public License (GPL)
3.0 [82]. All the required STL-rendered components were 3-D printed polylactic acid (PLA), ﬁlament of
diameter 2.85 mm on a Lulzbot TAZ 6 (Aleph Objects, Loveland CO). The objects were sliced with
Cura Lulzbot edition v.3.6.20 [83] using the settings detailed in Table 2. The optional cover was 3-D
printed translucent glycol modiﬁed polyethylene terephthalate (PETG) ﬁlament of diameter 2.85 mm
on a Lulzbot TAZ 6 [84].

 Abstract: This study provides designs for a low-cost, easily replicable open-source lab-grade digital
 scale that can be used as a precision balance. The design is such that it can be manufactured for use in
 most labs throughout the world with open-source RepRap-class material extrusion-based 3-D printers
 for the mechanical components and readily available open-source electronics including the Arduino
 Nano. Several versions of the design were fabricated and tested for precision and accuracy for a range
 of load cells. The results showed the open-source scale was found to be repeatable within 0.05 g with
 multiple load cells, with even better precision (0.005 g) depending on load cell range and style. The
 scale tracks linearly with proprietary lab-grade scales, meeting the performance speciﬁed in the load
  cell data sheets, indicating that it is accurate across the range of the load cell installed. The smallest
 load cell tested (100 g) oﬀers precision on the order of a commercial digital mass balance. The scale
 can be produced at signiﬁcant cost savings compared to scales of comparable range and precision
 when serial capability is present. The cost savings increase signiﬁcantly as the range of the scale
 increases and are particularly well-suited for resource-constrained medical and scientiﬁc facilities.


 Keywords: 3-D printing; additive manufacturing; distributed manufacturing; laboratory equipment;
 open hardware; open source; open-source hardware; scale; balance; mass




1. Introduction

    The incredible success of free and open-source development of software [1,2] is being rapidly
adopted by the hardware community [3,4] as it enables scientiﬁc equipment designers to rapidly build
upon one another’s work [5–7]. This has resulted in a democratization of design building on open-source
designs [8–10] often with open-source tools that enable a true digital distributed manufacturing [11–14].
The most robust examples, is the self-replicating rapid prototype (RepRap) fused ﬁlament fabrication
(FFF)-based 3-D printer [15–17] that has brought the cost of both rapid prototyping and additive
manufacturing low enough to be used by the masses [18–21]. FFF-based 3-D printers derived from
RepRaps now have 3-D printed parts with suﬃcient mechanical strength [22] to be used for ﬁnal
functional parts even if printed on machines that only cost a few hundred dollars. This has resulted in
an explosion of open-source digitally fabricated instruments and a repository of designs housed at
the NIH [5,6,23,24]. There are many examples of progressively more sophisticated open-source 3-D
printed parts being used to build chemical mixing systems [22–25], mechanical components for optics
setups [26–31] and microscopes [32–34], instruments to test water quality testing [35–38], various types
of syringe pumps [39–43] that are combined with other components to make complete systems for


Instruments 2020, 4, 18; doi:10.3390/instruments4030018                              www.mdpi.com/journal/instrumentsInstruments 2020, 4, 18                                                                                      2 of 26



making microﬂuidics and metaﬂuidics [44–47]. Although the most important features of open-source
3-D printable instruments is the ease with which scientists can customize a tool, in general, they are
also much less expensive than equivalent (and often technically inferior) commercial proprietary
systems [6,23,24,48–50] and provide a high return on investment [51,52]. This advantage expands as
the device is made with a higher percentage of digitally replicable components [53]. To continue this
tradition of standing on the shoulders of open hardware giants [54], this paper describes the design of
an open-source largely 3-D printed digital scale. A digital scale is a scientiﬁc instrument that provides
fast measurements of mass generally with an accuracy range of 0.1 g to 0.01 g and have easy-to-read
automatic liquid crystal displays. (Accuracy refers to closeness of the measurements to a known value
of the mass. On the other hand, precision refers to the closeness of the measurements to one another,
which is commonly stated as a single standard deviation of several tests and is independent of the
accuracy (i.e., statistical dispersion).) Digital scales are used in a wide range of scientiﬁc applications
including chemical research, genomics, drug discovery, and proteomics.  Scales are classiﬁed by
precision; where a precision balance has a measurement resolution of 0.001 g, an analytical balance has
one of 0.0001 g and a micro balance has one of 0.0000001 g. On the high-end of scales, an open-source
quartz crystal microbalance (OpenQCM) [55] uses an open-source Arduino Nano and is already well
established in the scientiﬁc literature [56–60].
     Thus, this study provides the designs for a low-cost, easily replicable, open-source lab-grade digital
scale that can be used as a precision balance depending on configuration. The design is such that it can
be manufactured for use in most labs throughout the world with open-source RepRap-class material
extrusion-based 3-D printers for the mechanical components and readily available open-source electronics
including the Arduino Nano. In addition, a validation procedure for quantifying the accuracy and
precision is provided. Several versions of the design were fabricated and tested for a range of load cells,
and the results are discussed in the context of resource-constrained medical and scientific facilities.

2. Materials and Methods

2.1. Design

    This series of open-source scales is based on load cells, which are devices designed to measure
weight or force. The load cells used in this study are strain gage load cells, which convert strain (i.e.,
change in length) of a material into an electrical signal proportional to the force applied [61]. This
series of scales was designed to be easily manufacturable, require minimal components, and oﬀer
the functionality necessary for a basic digital scale, a precision balance, and an analytical balance.
Component location is accomplished entirely by features on the 3-D printed components including
bosses and snap-ﬁt joints, meaning that the only fasteners required are those that hold the load cell in
place. The assembly was designed to fully enclose all electronics, limiting airﬂow, which has been
observed to aﬀect the output of the load cell ampliﬁer.
    At the time of writing, the scale is designed to accommodate two sizes of single-point parallel
beam load cells—TAL220 and TAL221—but is set up to enable fast adaptation to other models. These
were selected ﬁrst due to their low cost, wide selection of weight ranges, and insensitivity to moment
loading on the bed, which aids in measurement repeatability [61]. The base and bed were designed to
be easily modiﬁed to accommodate other load cell styles as required.
    The open-source scale systems were designed for use in two settings: (1) as an independent digital
scale with a displayed output which can be tared (the reference for a reading of zero can be oﬀset
to ignore a known mass, such as that of a container) and calibrated (the output can be corrected to
present the true mass by loading the scale with a known mass) without use of a computer, and (2) as a
serially connected logging instrument for a laboratory setting and data-logging. The serial capability
also allows this scale to be constructed without an LCD, oﬀering a signiﬁcant cost reduction for uses
that do not require a display, such as automated weighing or cases where a computer will always be
used as the power supply.Instruments 2020, 4, 18                                                                                      3 of 26



    To accomplish independent functionality, components were speciﬁed to ensure that the entire scale
can be powered by a 5V USB power supply, either from a computer USB port or cell phone charging
block, both of which are commonly available at low cost. The components’ power requirements are small
enough that the low-current digital pins on an Arduino can power them, allowing independent control

The excerpt describes the transition from early AI4EDA—where deep-learning models from other fields are repurposed for EDA tasks like PPA estimation, optimization, verification, and fault detection—to a proposed AI-native approach centered on large circuit models (LCMs). Multimodal foundation models (handling text, images, audio) and recent capabilities (e.g., GPT-4V-like systems) motivate integrating diverse circuit data. LCMs are envisioned to more fully encapsulate electrical, logical, and physical aspects, potentially revolutionizing design, optimization, and verification. The text notes that public-domain circuit data exist to train robust models despite proprietary barriers, and highlights architectural fits: Transformers and graph neural networks map well to circuit and schematic graph structure. It also emphasizes module reuse (arithmetic units, decoders, cryptographic cores) as an inductive bias favorable to ML. Current limitations of AI4EDA include acting as isolated analytical tools and insufficiently synthesizing multimodal circuit semantics, motivating research into unimodal representation learning and multimodal integration, and outlining challenges such as data scarcity and scalability.

The excerpt describes the difficulty of using powerful EDA tools like KiCad due to steep learning curves and fragmented documentation. It introduces SmartonAI, an AI-assisted interaction system that integrates large language models into the EDA workflow and is initially integrated with KiCad. SmartonAI consists of two complementary components: a Chat Plugin for multi-turn dialogue that decomposes tasks and retrieves documentation, and a OneCommandLine Plugin that recommends and executes relevant plugins based on user intent. The system leverages LLM capabilities to convert vague, high-level user requests into concrete design operations, and uses retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to ground outputs in documentation and plugin content. The paper situates this work within broader LLM advances (GPT-4.1, Claude, Gemini, LLaMA-3, etc.) and agent/tool orchestration frameworks (HuggingGPT, LangChain), noting successes in software development and multimodal agents. However, it also emphasizes that applying LLMs to domain-specific engineering tools like EDA remains underexplored because EDA workflows have intricate procedural logic, structured hierarchies, and tightly coupled UI actions that pose unique challenges. Preliminary results claim SmartonAI reduces onboarding time and enhances productivity.

A fully automated deep-learning pipeline for SEM image analysis is presented, using a Faster R-CNN (ResNet-50) for stitching misalignment detection and a VGG-16 regression model for stacking alignment, with synthetic data generation to reduce labeling costs. The text advocates model reusability by operating on feature images (vias/metal lines) rather than raw SEM images to improve generalization. Integration themes include co-design of algorithms and hardware, human-in-the-loop workflows, and yield-aware DTCO. It also describes ShortCircuit, an open-source toolchain that links ChatGPT-style NLP to EDA flows (OpenLane), demonstrating a move toward "specification-to-silicon" automation and NLP-driven, demand-oriented design. Examples of specialized hardware (TPU MAC arrays) illustrate compute acceleration for AI-driven EDA tasks.

The excerpt argues that current AI usage in EDA has largely been an “AI4EDA” approach that repurposes models from other domains and thus fails to capture the intertwined electrical, logical, and physical characteristics of circuits. The authors call for a shift to AI-native EDA built around Large Circuit Models (LCMs): multimodal foundation models trained to represent and align information across stages (specification, architecture, HLS/HDL, netlist, floorplan, layout). Each design stage is treated as a separate modality, with higher-level stages carrying more semantics and lower-level stages more detailed structure. Central promises of LCMs include fusing disparate representations across the design continuum to streamline the EDA flow, enable 'shift-left' design, and improve PPA. The paper also notes a practical challenge: many new designs resemble past ones but require subtle changes, highlighting the need for models that can leverage historical successes and failures. Overall, the field has only begun to explore foundation-model capabilities for circuit design, indicating both opportunity and a gap in tailored, multimodal AI solutions for EDA.

The excerpt surveys ML/AI methods used in analog IC design automation. Graph neural networks (GNNs) are highlighted for EDA tasks because circuit netlists are naturally graph-structured; GNNs enable representation learning on circuit structures and can model relations between components for tasks such as parasitic prediction and layout-aware optimization. Examples include MLParest (Random Forest on schematic features), BagNet (Berkeley Analog Generator with Layout Optimizer), ParaGraph (custom message passing for layout-dependent parasitics), and CktGNN (two-level/nested GNN encoding subgraphs). Topology-aware approaches use VAEs and dataset/benchmark efforts (OCB, ACOB) for topology exploration and op-amp generation. Large language models (LLMs) are being applied at the schematic/code-generation and automation level: AnalogCoder (PySpice code generation), SPICEPilot (prompt-steering to create PySpice), ADO-LLM (BO + LLM in-context learning for sizing), and AmpAgent (LLM multi-agent system). The paper notes LLM limitations in precise mathematical reasoning and the need to generate verifiable code; many methods focus on schematic-level verification and post-layout/parasitic effects remain a major concern.

The Guver et al. SECM project emphasizes open-source electronics and low-cost design: a DStat potentiostat (selected for 600 fA / 0.6 pA low-current capability) was used and built "based on an open source project" with a Python GUI. Motion was implemented with stepper motors driven from an Arduino controller, coupled mechanically via a manual XY micromanipulator stage and 3D-printed couplers/gears; motion control used "open source motor control software" (keywords include GRBL). The authors contrast cheap Arduino-based potentiostats (limited to high-microampere ranges and lacking SWV) with the DStat’s low-current performance, noting the full custom-built hardware cost was under $300. The excerpt documents the choice of modular, open-source components (Arduino, DStat, GRBL) and use of 3D-printed mechanical parts, but it does not provide specific PCB modularization strategies, explicit motor driver IC choices, nor detailed design-for-manufacture (DFM) practices. Where relevant, the paper points readers to existing open-source instructions (for DStat) rather than describing custom PCB layout or manufacturing workflows in this excerpt.

## pqac-00000041

From text 'booeshaghi2019designprinciplesfor pages 5-7'.

A. Sina Booeshaghi, Eduardo da Veiga Beltrame, Dylan Bannon, Jase Gehring, and Lior Pachter. Design principles for open source bioinstrumentation: the poseidon syringe pump system as an example. bioRxiv, Jan 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1101/521096, doi:10.1101/521096. This article has 7 citations.

The excerpt describes design principles from the poseidon open-source syringe pump and USB microscope system that are directly relevant to electronics and manufacturability. The authors emphasize standardization and use of widely available components: “Almost all of the components used in the poseidon system can be found in open source desktop 3D printers,” which supports design-for-manufacture and easy sourcing. For motor control the design uses a CNC shield with configurable microstepping: “By physically adding or removing the microstepping jumpers on the CNC shield board users can access a range of flow rates,” indicating reliance on common motor-driver hardware and simple hardware-configurable microstepping rather than bespoke driver PCBs. Modularity is highlighted (“modular, highly customizable syringe pump array”), and the project uses version control (GitHub) for collaborative development and documentation of PCB/firmware changes. The authors stress thorough user documentation (videos, photos, labeled instructions) to reduce assembly errors and speed adoption. They also recommend benchmarking against commercial systems and clearly documenting limitations. These points imply design practices: reuse common, standardized parts and connectors, prefer off-the-shelf driver boards for motor control with user-accessible jumpers, keep the system modular, and provide strong version-controlled documentation to aid manufacture and modification.

What are the recommended licensing, documentation standards, and quality monitoring approaches?", "text_name_focus_list": ["nkoudou2018globalopenscience pages 19-25", "nkoudou2018globalopenscience pages 25-29", "wenzel2023openhardwarefrom pages 5-9", "wenzel2023openhardwarefrom pages 9-13"]}

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000042

From text 'nkoudou2018globalopenscience pages 29-34'.

TH Mboa Nkoudou, D Bild, F Grey, and H Toivonen. Global open science hardware roadmap: making open science hardware ubiquitous by 2025. Unknown journal, 2018.

The excerpt recommends that Open Science Hardware (OScH) projects adopt Free and Open Source licensing and be accompanied by high-quality, well-documented materials so users and developers can learn, build and create derivatives. It emphasizes establishing platforms and tools to leverage community participation, producing community-contributed lists of resources, and creating clear build instructions and documentation standards. On design-for-manufacture and scale-up, the roadmap suggests identifying production partners, incubators/accelerators, and investment partners (a network of recommended production and funding models) to support manufacturing and distribution. For licensing and business approach it notes attention to "licensing strategies, business models" and recommends funder guidance that preferentially supports open projects. For quality monitoring and evaluation it calls for "appropriate metrics and evaluation methods" to compare open vs proprietary impact, periodic updates to guidance with benchmarks, case studies, and sharing funders' experiences to track progress and demonstrate social, economic, and research benefits.


---

## pqac-00000043

From text 'nkoudou2018globalopenscience pages 19-25'.

TH Mboa Nkoudou, D Bild, F Grey, and H Toivonen. Global open science hardware roadmap: making open science hardware ubiquitous by 2025. Unknown journal, 2018.

The GOSH roadmap emphasizes open, clear documentation and context-aware quality monitoring rather than a one-size-fits-all metric. For quality monitoring it recommends building a common pool of open data on OScH projects, creating contextualized metrics for impact assessment and encouraging funders to adopt them, and engaging STS researchers to study socio-technical contexts. Monitoring frameworks should be flexible and grounded in local socio-technical, economic and political conditions, enabling product- or context-specific metrics, collaborative metric design, and open-data-based evaluation. On licensing and documentation, the roadmap stresses that open hardware licensing is complex and calls for convening community members, researchers, businesses and lawyers to improve licensing and contracting strategies and to produce contract templates (e.g., following ContractPatch). It endorses core open-hardware norms (from OSHWA): allow anyone to study, modify, distribute, make, and sell the hardware; provide publicly accessible design files and documentation; clearly state what portion is released under license; and avoid misleading claims about derivative products, trademark misuse, or releasing work as non-commercial/no-derivatives. It also notes optional practices such as requiring attribution or reciprocal licensing and warns of appropriation risks (e.g., Makerbot).


---

## pqac-00000044

From text 'nkoudou2018globalopenscience pages 1-12'.

TH Mboa Nkoudou, D Bild, F Grey, and H Toivonen. Global open science hardware roadmap: making open science hardware ubiquitous by 2025. Unknown journal, 2018.

The excerpt indicates the GOSH roadmap recommends creating explicit, community-endorsed guidelines covering licensing, documentation, and quality control for Open Science Hardware (OScH). It calls for preparing guidance for hardware designers, funders, users and newcomers on “quality control and standards compliance, licensing, documentation standards, and social and ethical aspects of scientific work.” The roadmap also identifies dedicated sections for “QUALITY MONITORING AND EVALUATION” and “OPEN HARDWARE LICENSING,” and urges the community’s involvement in elaborating an assessment framework for OScH projects. Additionally, it promotes using collaborative research outputs to build a shared pool of open educational resources and establishing mentorship and support networks. The document frames OScH as hardware that must be obtainable, assemblable, usable, inspectable, modifiable, shareable, and sellable, implying documentation and design practices should enable reproducibility and manufacturability. However, the provided pages do not list specific licenses, documentation templates, or detailed design-for-manufacture practices; they set high-level expectations and call for community processes to define assessment, licensing, and documentation standards.

## pqac-00000045

From text 'nkoudou2018globalopenscience pages 44-51'.

TH Mboa Nkoudou, D Bild, F Grey, and H Toivonen. Global open science hardware roadmap: making open science hardware ubiquitous by 2025. Unknown journal, 2018.

The excerpt highlights gaps and recommended community actions rather than detailed technical norms. It emphasizes limited mainstream access to Open Science Hardware (OScH) and notes that hobbyist digital fabrication, maker spaces, and open platforms have lowered barriers. To increase availability, the roadmap recommends building OScH distributor networks and creating resource-sharing best practices to scale and contextualize manufacturing and decentralized distribution. It also identifies an opportunity to build local manufacturing capacity to serve markets underserved by commercial suppliers. As milestone outputs, the roadmap expects a common pool of open educational resources and "Open Hardware documentation and licensing guidelines" to be produced and widely adopted, supporting institutional and community uptake. The footnotes point readers to existing definitions and licensing resources (e.g., OSHWA Open Source Hardware Definition, Solderpad licenses, OpenMTA) and to practical guides and books for DIY manufacturing. The excerpt does not provide specific documentation templates, explicit design-for-manufacture practices, nor detailed quality-monitoring procedures; it frames those as areas where community-developed guidelines and best practices should be produced and adopted.

## pqac-00000046

From text 'nkoudou2018globalopenscience pages 25-29'.

TH Mboa Nkoudou, D Bild, F Grey, and H Toivonen. Global open science hardware roadmap: making open science hardware ubiquitous by 2025. Unknown journal, 2018.

The provided excerpt from the GOSH roadmap emphasizes institutional measures to support open science hardware (OScH) rather than giving detailed technical norms. It explicitly recommends that technology transfer offices adopt recognized open‑hardware licensing and material transfer mechanisms — naming TAPR OHL, CERN OHL, the Solderpad license and the OpenMTA — and that OScH be included in broader Open Science policy discussions to improve incentives and credit. The roadmap also calls for direct, practical support through shared fabrication facilities (hackerspaces, makerspaces, Fab Labs) combined with professional engineering help, and notes that tools and community networks for sharing "high‑quality hardware documentation" are emerging but need more support. The excerpt does not provide specific documentation templates, design‑for‑manufacture (DfM) practices, nor explicit quality‑monitoring procedures; it frames those needs at a policy and institutional support level and points to the need for further standards and infrastructure to be developed elsewhere in the roadmap.

## pqac-00000047

From text 'miljkovic2109towardsfairprinciples pages 8-9'.

Nadica Miljković, Ana Trisovic, and Limor Peer. Towards fair principles for open hardware. Text, Jan 2109. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2109.06045, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2109.06045. This article has 7 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt recommends FAIR-aligned documentation and design-for-manufacture practices for open hardware: assign globally unique persistent identifiers (e.g., DOI or OSHWA IDs) and include them in metadata; register/index hardware in searchable repositories; store OH files cohesively in repository infrastructure to support long-term stewardship. Metadata should be rich and reflect complex hardware structure, including clear and accessible usage licenses applied to each component and compatible dependencies. Documentation should include detailed provenance and bill of materials (BOM), assembly instructions, and cross-references to software, data, and documentation. Use shared vocabularies that follow FAIR and domain community standards to enable interoperability. Beyond FAIR, emphasize maintainability, version control, and quality control as important qualities. The excerpt also notes ongoing standardization efforts (e.g., DIN SPEC 3105) and calls for good documentation practices to enable reuse and findability. These points address identifiers, licensing at component level, documentation standards (BOM, assembly instructions, rich metadata, vocabularies), repository practices, and quality/maintenance monitoring, but the text is from Miljković et al. and not the GOSH roadmap itself.

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive reference summary table with 14 references covering three topics: (a) open-hardware electronics design practice and reproducibility, (b) modular motor/sensor control electronics and design-for-manufacture, and (c) generative/automated EDA. For each reference include: Reference # (1-14), Full Citation, Topic Category (a/b/c), 3-5 Sentence Summary, and Relevance to Multi-Channel Powder Doser PCB Design.

The 14 references should be:
1. Booeshaghi et al. (2019) - Poseidon syringe pump, Scientific Reports
2. Stirling et al. (2022) - HardOps for OpenFlexure, Int J Computer Integrated Manufacturing
3. GOSH Community (2018) - Global Open Science Hardware Roadmap
4. Hohlbein et al. (2022) - Open microscopy quo vadis, Nature Methods
5. Subbaraman et al. (2024) - Duckbot/Jubilee, PLOS ONE
6. Wenzel (2023) - Open hardware from DIY to global transformation, PLOS Biology
7. Fischer et al. (2025) - Modular multi-axis stepper motor driver, arXiv
8. Hubbard & Pearce (2020) - Open-source lab-grade scales, Instruments
9. Hernández-del-Valle et al. (2024) - Pellet dispensomixer, Digital Discovery
10. Guver et al. (2019) - Low-cost SECM with open-source tools, HardwareX
11. Keller et al. (2023) - Open science hardware Wikimedia fellowships, Research Ideas and Outcomes
12. Huang et al. (2021) - ML for EDA survey, ACM TODAES
13. Chen et al. (2024) - Dawn of AI-native EDA / Large Circuit Models, arXiv
14. Han et al. (2023/2025) - SmartonAI for KiCad, arXiv/ICML 2025"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Ref # | Full Citation | Topic Category | 3-5 Sentence Summary | Relevance to Multi-Channel Powder Doser PCB Design |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Booeshaghi, A. S., da Veiga Beltrame, E., Bannon, D., Gehring, J., & Pachter, L. (2019). *Principles of open source bioinstrumentation applied to the poseidon syringe pump system*. Scientific Reports, 9. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-48815-9 | a | This paper frames open scientific hardware around functionality, robustness, safety, simplicity, modularity, benchmarking, and documentation. Poseidon combines off-the-shelf electronics with 3D-printed mechanics and uses an Arduino plus CNC shield for pump actuation, with a Raspberry Pi/touchscreen stack for the microscope side. The authors stress that adoption depends not just on working hardware, but on clear BOMs, build guidance, cross-platform software, and careful end-to-end testing of hardware/software interactions such as microstepping settings. It is one of the clearest peer-reviewed exemplars of practical open-bioinstrument design norms. (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000007) | Strongly relevant as a template for a powder doser architecture using commodity controller boards and modular actuators. It suggests using standard modules first, documenting exact part numbers and firmware settings, and benchmarking dispensing performance against commercial or manual methods. Its emphasis on simplicity and robust documentation maps directly to manufacturable, reproducible control PCBs or controller stacks for multiple powder channels. |
| 2 | Stirling, J., Bumke, K., Collins, J., Dhokia, V., & Bowman, R. (2022). *HardOps: utilising the software development toolchain for hardware design*. International Journal of Computer Integrated Manufacturing, 35(12), 1297-1309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0951192X.2022.2028188 | a | HardOps adapts software-style DevOps workflows to hardware projects, using distributed version control, issue tracking, CI pipelines, and linked repositories to make hardware development more reproducible. Using OpenFlexure as the example, the paper argues that design files, documentation, production outputs, planning records, and test feedback should all live in an integrated, versioned workflow. This closes the loop between design, manufacture, and verification rather than treating documentation as an afterthought. The paper is especially important because it addresses process infrastructure, not just the physical instrument. (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024) | Highly relevant for a powder doser PCB program because it points to the missing layer in many open-hardware projects: controlled release of schematics, Gerbers, BOMs, firmware, test procedures, and manufacturing revisions. For a multi-channel board, HardOps implies CI-like checks on source files, versioned manufacturing outputs, and traceable issue histories for failures such as missed steps, noisy HX711 readings, or assembly defects. |
| 3 | Nkoudou, T. H. M., Bild, D., Grey, F., & Toivonen, H. (2018). *Global open science hardware roadmap: making open science hardware ubiquitous by 2025*. GOSH Community roadmap. | a | The GOSH roadmap is a foundational community document defining open science hardware as hardware that can be obtained, assembled, used, studied, modified, shared, and sold by anyone. It emphasizes that documentation, quality evaluation, licensing, and local manufacturability are not optional extras but prerequisites for reproducibility and equitable access. The roadmap recommends recognized open-hardware licenses, community documentation norms, contextual quality metrics, and support structures such as fabrication facilities and production partners. It is less a device paper than a governance and infrastructure roadmap for reproducible scientific hardware. (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000046) | Relevant because a multi-powder doser meant to be easily replicated needs more than a working PCB: it needs license clarity, assembly instructions, test criteria, and a path to local fabrication. The roadmap supports designing PCBs and firmware so others can source, build, inspect, and adapt them in different regions and manufacturing ecosystems. |
| 4 | Hohlbein, J., Diederich, B., Marsikova, B., Reynaud, E. G., Holden, S., Jahr, W., Haase, R., & Prakash, K. (2022). *Open microscopy in the life sciences: quo vadis?* Nature Methods, 19(9), 1020-1025. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41592-022-01602-3 | a | This Nature Methods comment synthesizes best practices for open microscopy, but its lessons generalize well to lab automation hardware. It stresses that open hardware should publish editable source design files rather than only derived exports, provide calibration references and sample standards, and build communities around support and maintenance. It also highlights the importance of plugin-oriented software/device interfaces and long-term interoperability. The paper is valuable as a mature statement of norms from an open-instrument subfield that has already grappled with reproducibility at scale. (pqac-00000005) | Relevant because a powder doser PCB should be released with editable CAD/schematic sources, not just PDFs or Gerbers, and should include calibration procedures for load cells and motor channels. The paper also argues for interface stability and modularity, which supports designing expandable sensor/actuator buses and test points for future accessories. |
| 5 | Subbaraman, B., de Lange, O., Ferguson, S., & Peek, N. (2024). *The Duckbot: A system for automated imaging and manipulation of duckweed*. PLOS ONE, 19, e0296717. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0296717 | a/b | Duckbot shows how the Jubilee open-source tool-changing motion platform can be repurposed for scientific automation through modular end-effectors, programmatic control, and reusable software abstractions. The system combines Duet-based motion electronics, a Raspberry Pi computer, notebook-driven control, OpenTrons-compatible labware definitions, and multiple interchangeable tools such as cameras, syringes, and pipettes. Its importance lies in showing that flexible scientific automation can be built around a stable motion/electronics backbone plus swappable toolheads. It bridges open hardware practice and modular electronics/software architecture. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000014) | Relevant because a multi-powder doser may benefit from the same architecture: one stable controller PCB/backplane with interchangeable dosing heads, weighing heads, or hoppers. Duckbot suggests separating the common motion/control electronics from application-specific tools and exposing them through a programmable API, which improves replication and future expansion. |
| 6 | Wenzel, T. (2023). *Open hardware: From DIY trend to global transformation in access to laboratory equipment*. PLOS Biology, 21(1), e3001931. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.3001931 | a | Wenzel’s essay situates open hardware as a transition from ad hoc DIY practice toward globally distributed, digitally shared laboratory technology. It highlights open sharing, standard parts, local production, digital fabrication, and strong documentation as central drivers of adoption. The article emphasizes that reducing dependence on imports and proprietary supply chains is as important as reducing headline cost. It is useful as a strategic overview of why open scientific hardware succeeds or fails in real deployment contexts. (pqac-00000031) | Relevant because a powder doser intended for broad replication should minimize exotic parts, rely on standard connectors and common microcontrollers, and be producible by low-volume PCB assemblers. The article supports choosing electronics that can be locally sourced and serviced, not just optimized for one laboratory. |
| 7 | Fischer, M. S., Grass, D., & Fischer, M. C. (2025). *Modular multi-axis stepper motor driver with remote control for use in microscopy*. arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2511.09456 | b | Fischer et al. describe a modular multi-axis controller built around Raspberry Pi Pico microcontrollers and Trinamic TMC driver boards. The design uses SPI to communicate with per-axis motor drivers, USB serial to a host computer, connectorized stage interfaces, and optional encoders and limit switches for open-loop or closed-loop operation. A notable design choice is the use of evaluation boards and prototyping hardware rather than a fully custom PCB, explicitly trading compactness for ease of construction and adaptability. The authors also identify future PCB integration opportunities to reduce soldering and cost. (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021) | Directly relevant to a powder doser with many auger or rotor channels. It offers a concrete reference for modular stepper control, encoder feedback, connector strategy, and the transition path from module-based prototype to manufacturable integrated PCB. |
| 8 | Hubbard, B. R., & Pearce, J. M. (2020). *Open-Source Digitally Replicable Lab-Grade Scales*. Instruments, 4(3), 18. https://doi.org/10.3390/instruments4030018 | b | This paper presents a digitally replicable lab-grade scale using an Arduino Nano, HX711 load-cell amplifier, and 3D-printed structure. The design supports both standalone operation and serial logging, and intentionally minimizes parts count while enclosing electronics to reduce airflow-induced measurement errors. The authors provide a detailed BOM, wiring diagrams, serial protocol details, and validation across different load cells. It is one of the most directly relevant open references for low-cost weighing electronics in scientific hardware. (pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031) | Extremely relevant for gravimetric feedback in a multi-powder doser. It informs selection and integration of HX711 front-ends, serial protocols, low-cost USB-powered electronics, and calibration workflows for per-channel or shared weighing stations. |
| 9 | Hernández-del-Valle, M., Ilarraza-Zuazo, J., Dios-Lázaro, E., Rubio, J., Audoux, J., & Haranczyk, M. (2024). *Pellet dispensomixer and pellet distributor: Open hardware for nanocomposite space exploration via automated material compounding*. Digital Discovery, 3, 2032-2040. https://doi.org/10.1039/d4dd00198b | b | This paper is the closest application match to a multi-material powder or pellet dosing problem. The dispensomixer uses multiple dispensers, stepper motors, Arduino-based control, serially connected balances for feedback, and routines that alternate motor directions to avoid clogging. The system is explicitly intended for materials experimentation and self-driving-lab workflows, and includes open CAD, control code, and BOM information. It demonstrates that multi-channel solid-material dispensing benefits from integrating weighing, actuation, and software orchestration rather than treating them as separate subsystems. (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027) | Directly relevant to the target device. It supports designing a controller PCB with multiple stepper channels, serial balance integration, anti-jam motor routines, and a host-controlled architecture that can be scaled to many ingredients. |
| 10 | Guver, A., Fifita, N., Milas, P., Straker, M., Guy, M., Green, K., Yildirim, T., Unlu, I., Yigit, M. V., & Ozturk, B. (2019). *A low-cost and high-precision scanning electrochemical microscope built with open source tools*. HardwareX, 6, e00082. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ohx.2019.e00082 | b | Guver et al. combine open-source electronics blocks into a capable scientific instrument: a DStat potentiostat, Arduino-controlled stepper motion, open motor-control software, and 3D-printed mechanics. The project illustrates a common open-hardware pattern of composing validated subsystems rather than custom-designing every PCB from scratch. It also reflects HardwareX-style norms of cost transparency and buildability. While not centered on PCB design itself, it is useful as an example of modular electronics integration. (pqac-00000040) | Relevant because a powder doser can likewise benefit from composing proven controller, sensor, and communications modules before committing to a custom integrated board. The paper reinforces the practical value of staged integration: module-based prototype first, then custom PCB only where it meaningfully improves cost, reliability, or assembly. |
| 11 | Keller, O., Appelhoff, S., Paffhausen, B., & Wenzel, T. (2023). *Development and Sharing of Open Science Hardware: Lessons Learned from Wikimedia Fellowships*. Research Ideas and Outcomes, 9. https://doi.org/10.3897/rio.9.e95174 | a/b | Keller et al. reflect on several open hardware projects and highlight recurring lessons: public repositories matter, modular boards and controller partitions help, but reproducibility is still hard when projects depend on poorly documented commodity modules or proprietary toolchains. They describe practical trade-offs between “flying cable” prototypes and PCB-based builds, and note the need to move from ad hoc prototypes to clearer, more maintainable electronics assemblies. The paper is especially good at exposing what remains difficult in real open-hardware development, not just what works in idealized examples. (pqac-00000003) | Relevant because a powder doser project will likely face the same transition from breadboards and module stacks to manufacturable boards. The paper argues for early repository discipline, explicit module boundaries, and realistic attention to sourcing, wiring, and proprietary dependencies when designing reproducible control electronics. |
| 12 | Huang, G., Hu, J., He, Y., Liu, J., Ma, M., Shen, Z., Wu, J., Xu, Y., Zhang, H., Zhong, K., Ning, X., Ma, Y., Yang, H., Yu, B., Yang, H., & Wang, Y. (2021). *Machine Learning for Electronic Design Automation: A Survey*. ACM Transactions on Design Automation of Electronic Systems, 26(5), 1-46. https://doi.org/10.1145/3451179 | c | This survey synthesizes the pre-LLM wave of ML-for-EDA, covering tasks such as estimation, optimization, verification, and other chip-design problems. Its main value here is diagnostic: it shows that most ML-for-EDA research has focused on IC/VLSI flows rather than board-level scientific-instrument electronics. The survey makes clear that AI in EDA has historically been task-specific and heavily domain-constrained. It therefore provides a baseline for judging how immature automated PCB design remains in open scientific hardware. | Relevant because it shows the gap: if one hopes for generative PCB design for a powder doser, the literature base is not on scientific instrument boards but on chip-level automation. The implication is that component selection, schematic partitioning, layout, and DFM for laboratory PCBs still largely require manual engineering. |
| 13 | Chen, L., Chen, Y., Chu, Z., Fang, W., Ho, T.-Y., Huang, R., Huang, Y., Khan, S., Li, M., Li, X., Li, Y., Liang, Y., Liu, J., Liu, Y., Lin, Y., Luo, G., Shi, Z., Sun, G., Tsaras, D., Wang, R., Wang, Z., Wei, X., Xie, Z., Xu, Q., Xue, C., Yan, J., Yang, J., Yu, B., Yuan, M., Young, E. F. Y., Zeng, X., Zhang, H., Zhang, Z., Zhao, Y., Zhen, H.-L., Zheng, Z., Zhu, B., Zhu, K., & Zou, S. (2024). *The Dawn of AI-Native EDA: Promises and Challenges of Large Circuit Models*. arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2403.07257 | c | Chen et al. argue that current AI4EDA methods are narrow and propose AI-native EDA built around large circuit models that jointly represent electrical, logical, and physical design information. The paper is ambitious and forward-looking, but again focused on integrated-circuit design abstractions such as specification, RTL, netlist, floorplan, and layout. It is valuable because it identifies what true generative EDA would require: multimodal training data, cross-stage alignment, and leverage of prior design histories. For scientific hardware, it highlights both promise and the present absence of analogous board-level foundation models. (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000035) | Relevant because a powder doser controller board would need exactly this sort of cross-stage reasoning—requirements, BOM, schematic, layout, firmware constraints, and manufacturability—but there is no comparable mature model for open lab-instrument PCBs. This paper therefore supports the conclusion that most such work remains manual today. |
| 14 | Han, B., Wang, X., Wang, Y., Yan, J., & Tian, Y. (2023/2025). *New Interaction Paradigm for Complex EDA Software Leveraging GPT*. arXiv / ICML 2025. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2307.14740 | c | SmartonAI is one of the few works directly touching open-source PCB tooling, specifically KiCad. Rather than automatically generating complete electronics designs, it uses LLMs and retrieval-augmented interaction to help users navigate documentation, decompose tasks, and invoke plugins through natural language. The work therefore lowers the skill barrier to using EDA software, but does not solve core engineering problems such as component choice, circuit topology, EMI-aware placement, or manufacturable routing. It is best understood as AI-assisted tooling, not generative electrical design. (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000033) | Highly relevant to the gap analysis. For a multi-channel powder doser, SmartonAI-like tools may help a designer work faster in KiCad, but the architecture, safety, load-cell front-end design, motor-power staging, connectorization, and DFM decisions still need human engineering judgment. |


*Table: This table summarizes 14 key references spanning open-hardware reproducibility, modular control electronics, and generative/automated EDA. It is useful as a compact literature map for designing a manufacturable, reproducible multi-channel powder doser control PCB.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033, pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000036, pqac-00000037, pqac-00000038, pqac-00000039, pqac-00000040, pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000044, pqac-00000045, pqac-00000046, pqac-00000047]

function.arguments:
{"answer": "## Electronics Design for Open-Source Scientific Hardware and Laboratory Automation: A Literature Review with Implications for a Multi-Channel Powder Dosing Device

### 1. Introduction

The design of low-cost, modular, microcontroller-driven control electronics for scientific instruments sits at the intersection of three rapidly evolving fields: open-source scientific hardware practice, modular motor/sensor electronics engineering, and emerging generative and automated electronic design automation (EDA). This review synthesizes 14 key references (2018–2025) spanning these domains, with explicit relevance to the design of manufacturable, easily replicated control PCBs for a multi-channel powder dosing device. The following sections are organized around the three requested topic areas, followed by a gap analysis of generative/automated EDA maturity.

### 2. Open-Hardware Electronics Design Practice and Reproducibility (Topic a)

The open scientific hardware community has developed a coherent set of design norms over the past decade, codified by projects, community roadmaps, and dedicated journals.

**Poseidon syringe pump system.** Booeshaghi et al. (2019) articulated seven design principles for open bioinstrumentation—functionality, robustness, safety, simplicity, modularity, benchmarking, and documentation—using the Poseidon open-source syringe pump as a worked example. The system uses an Arduino Uno with a CNC shield driving up to four stepper-motor pumps, paired with a Raspberry Pi and touchscreen for microscope control. The total cost is under $400 and assembly takes less than one hour. The authors emphasize that adoption depends on precise BOMs with vendor links, build videos, cross-platform executables, and firmware loaded via Arduino IDE (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000007). Critically, the electronics architecture relies entirely on off-the-shelf boards rather than custom PCBs, using configurable microstepping jumpers on the CNC shield to set flow-rate ranges (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000041).

**HardOps for OpenFlexure.** Stirling et al. (2022) introduced HardOps, adapting software DevOps workflows—distributed version control, CI pipelines, issue tracking, and linked multi-repository management—to hardware design. The OpenFlexure microscope project maintains separate repositories for mechanical hardware, software, electronics, embedded OS, project website, and failure mode analysis, all integrated through GitLab CI pipelines that can automatically generate production files and track feedback from distributed manufacturing and testing (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024). This approach is significant because it addresses process infrastructure rather than just device design, enabling reproducible manufacturing across multiple sites and countries.

**GOSH Roadmap.** The Global Open Science Hardware community roadmap (2018) defines open science hardware as hardware that can be "obtained, assembled, used, studied, modified, shared, and sold by anyone" and recommends recognized licenses (CERN OHL, TAPR OHL, Solderpad), documentation standards, contextual quality metrics, and support structures including shared fabrication facilities (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000046). The roadmap is governance-oriented rather than technical, but its norms directly shape how control PCBs should be documented and released.

**Open microscopy norms.** Hohlbein et al. (2022) synthesized best practices in a Nature Methods comment, stressing that open hardware should publish editable source files (not just STL or PDF exports), provide calibration references, and build plugin-oriented software interfaces for long-term device interoperability. They also emphasized that distributing hardware is fundamentally harder than distributing software because of non-zero replication cost and assembly complexity (pqac-00000005).

**Jubilee and Science-Jubilee.** Subbaraman et al. (2024) demonstrated the Duckbot, a plant biology automation system built on the Jubilee open-source tool-changing motion platform. Jubilee uses Duet3 controller boards running RepRap firmware, with a Raspberry Pi 4 for higher-level computation and notebook-driven experiment control. The system supports interchangeable 3D-printed and laser-cut tools (pipettes, cameras, syringes) attached via a tool-changing mechanism, with the Science-Jubilee Python package wrapping G-code control and exposing OpenTrons-compatible labware definitions (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013). The base kit costs approximately $1,700 and can be assembled from off-the-shelf components.

**From DIY to global transformation.** Wenzel (2023) situated open hardware as a transition from ad hoc maker projects to globally distributed laboratory technology, emphasizing that reducing dependence on import logistics and proprietary supply chains is as important as headline cost reduction. Central drivers of adoption include open sharing, digital fabrication, standard parts, local production, and detailed documentation (pqac-00000031).

**Lessons from Wikimedia fellowships.** Keller et al. (2023) reflected on practical open-hardware development, noting recurring challenges in transitioning from "flying cable" prototypes to reproducible PCB-based builds. They described trade-offs between custom controller boards and reliance on poorly documented commodity modules, and stressed the need for early repository discipline, explicit module boundaries, and realistic attention to sourcing and proprietary dependencies (pqac-00000003).

### 3. Modular and Expandable Motor/Sensor Control Electronics (Topic b)

**Modular multi-axis stepper driver.** Fischer et al. (2025) described a low-cost (~$360) open-source multi-axis stepper controller for microscopy, built around a Raspberry Pi Pico 2 microcontroller and up to four Trinamic TMC5240 evaluation boards communicating via SPI. The system supports per-axis encoders and limit switches via DB-9 connectors, implements open-loop, open-loop with pull-in correction, and closed-loop control modes, and includes a separate remote controller with joystick and rotary encoder inputs linked via UART over RJ-45. Software includes C and Python libraries and a µManager driver. The authors explicitly note that they chose evaluation boards over custom PCBs to simplify construction, but propose future PCB integration to reduce soldering and cost (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021). This staged approach—module-based prototype first, custom PCB second—is a recurring pattern in open scientific hardware.

**Open-source lab-grade scales.** Hubbard and Pearce (2020) provided a thoroughly documented open-source digital scale using an Arduino Nano, HX711 load-cell amplifier, and 3D-printed enclosure. The design achieves 0.05 g repeatability (0.005 g with small-range load cells), supports serial logging compliant with the SMA SCP-0499 protocol, and can operate standalone with LCD or as a USB-connected logging instrument. Component placement uses entirely 3D-printed snap-fit features, minimizing fasteners (pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031). This is directly relevant to gravimetric feedback in multi-channel powder dosing.

**Pellet dispensomixer.** Hernández-del-Valle et al. (2024) presented the closest published analog to a multi-material powder dosing system: an eight-channel pellet dispensomixer using Arduino MEGA-controlled stepper motors with A4988 drivers, an external balance for mass-flow feedback, and iteratively designed 3D-printed rotors with anti-clogging features. The system costs approximately €250 and is intended for automated nanocomposite compounding and self-driving laboratory workflows. Control code communicates via serial with a host computer running Python notebooks that orchestrate dispensing and balance feedback (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027).

**Open-source SECM.** Guver et al. (2019) demonstrated a scanning electrochemical microscope built under $300 from an open-source DStat potentiostat, Arduino-controlled stepper motors with driver shields, GRBL motion control software, and 3D-printed mechanics. The project exemplifies HardwareX publication norms of cost transparency, open source files, and clear buildability instructions (pqac-00000040).

### 4. Generative and Automated EDA for Scientific Instruments (Topic c)

**ML for EDA survey.** Huang et al. (2021) provided a comprehensive pre-LLM survey of machine learning applications in electronic design automation, covering estimation, optimization, verification, and other tasks across the IC design hierarchy. Critically, the entire survey focuses on VLSI/integrated-circuit flows; board-level PCB design for scientific instruments is not addressed (pqac-00000032).

**Large Circuit Models.** Chen et al. (2024) proposed AI-native EDA built around Large Circuit Models (LCMs)—multimodal foundation models that would jointly represent specification, RTL, netlist, floorplan, and layout information. While ambitious, the proposal is entirely framed around integrated-circuit design abstractions. There is no analogous effort for board-level scientific instrument PCBs (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000035).

**Generative AI for analog IC design.** Zadeh and Elamien (2025) surveyed the application of GNNs, VAEs, and LLMs to analog circuit sizing and topology exploration, highlighting tools such as AnalogCoder and AmpAgent. These approaches remain focused on IC-level design and do not address board-level instrument electronics (pqac-00000037).

**SmartonAI for KiCad.** Han et al. (2023/2025) developed SmartonAI, an LLM-assisted interaction plugin for KiCad that uses retrieval-augmented generation and task decomposition to help users navigate documentation, invoke plugins, and execute design operations through natural language. This is the only published work directly touching open-source PCB tooling with AI assistance. However, SmartonAI assists with tool interaction rather than generating circuit designs: it lowers the skill barrier to using KiCad but does not solve component selection, circuit topology, EMI-aware placement, or manufacturable routing (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000033).

### 5. Gap Analysis: How Mature Is Generative/Automated Electrical Design for Open Scientific Hardware?

The evidence reveals a stark gap between the ambitions of generative EDA research and the practical needs of open scientific hardware electronics designers. The key findings are:

1. **All published ML/AI EDA work targets VLSI/IC flows.** The surveys by Huang et al. (2021), Chen et al. (2024), Zadeh and Elamien (2025), and Xu et al. (2025) exclusively address chip-level design—standard cell placement, routing, parasitic extraction, and verification. Board-level PCB design for laboratory instruments is entirely absent from this literature (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000037, pqac-00000039).

2. **SmartonAI is the closest tool to open-hardware PCB automation, but it is an assistant, not a generator.** It helps users interact with KiCad through natural language but does not create schematics, select components, or produce layouts (pqac-00000033).

3. **Open-hardware projects avoid custom PCB design by using off-the-shelf modules.** Poseidon uses Arduino + CNC shield (pqac-00000001); Fischer et al. use TMC evaluation boards (pqac-00000021); Hernández-del-Valle et al. use Arduino MEGA + A4988 driver modules (pqac-00000025); Hubbard and Pearce use Arduino Nano + HX711 breakout (pqac-00000030). This module-stacking approach is effective for prototyping but creates reproducibility and reliability issues at scale, as noted by Keller et al. (pqac-00000003).

4. **What remains entirely manual** for scientific hardware PCBs includes: requirements-to-schematic translation, component selection and sourcing, schematic capture, power-integrity and signal-integrity analysis, PCB layout and routing, design-rule checking, Gerber generation, and DFM review. The HardOps CI pipeline automates downstream steps (building CAD, generating documentation) but not the core electrical design (pqac-00000024).

5. **The transition from module prototype to integrated PCB is a known bottleneck.** Fischer et al. explicitly propose future PCB integration as a manufacturing improvement (pqac-00000021), and Keller et al. describe the difficulty of moving from flying-wire prototypes to reliable, documented boards (pqac-00000003).

In summary, generative or automated electrical design for open scientific hardware is essentially nonexistent as a fielded capability. The AI/EDA community is making rapid progress on IC-level automation, but board-level instrument PCB design—the kind needed for a multi-channel powder doser—remains a manual engineering discipline. LLM-assisted tools like SmartonAI may reduce learning curves, but architecture, safety, analog front-end design, motor-power staging, connector strategy, and design-for-manufacture decisions require human judgment for the foreseeable future.

### 6. Reference Summary Table

The following table provides full citations, summaries, and explicit relevance mappings for all 14 references surveyed.

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

### 7. Implications for Multi-Channel Powder Doser PCB Design

Based on this literature, a manufacturable control PCB for a multi-channel powder dosing device should:

- **Adopt Poseidon-style design principles**: functionality, robustness, simplicity, modularity, benchmarking, and documentation (pqac-00000002).
- **Use a Jubilee/Duckbot-inspired architecture**: stable controller backbone with swappable dosing channel modules and a Python API for host-side orchestration (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000012).
- **Follow the Fischer et al. staged approach**: prototype with commodity modules (Arduino/Pico + TMC driver boards + HX711 breakouts), then consolidate onto an integrated PCB for manufacturing (pqac-00000021).
- **Integrate gravimetric feedback** using the Hubbard & Pearce HX711 + load-cell architecture and serial logging protocol (pqac-00000030).
- **Reference the pellet dispensomixer** for multi-channel motor control, anti-jam routines, and balance integration patterns (pqac-00000025).
- **Apply HardOps CI workflows** for version-controlled schematics, automated BOM/Gerber generation, and traceable manufacturing revisions (pqac-00000024).
- **Release design files** per GOSH and OSHWA norms: editable KiCad sources, BOM with vendor links, assembly instructions, calibration procedures, and recognized open-hardware licenses (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000042, pqac-00000005).
- **Expect fully manual electrical design** for the PCB itself, with potential assistance from LLM-based tools for KiCad interaction but not for core engineering decisions (pqac-00000033)."}

### 1. Introduction

The design of low-cost, modular, microcontroller-driven control electronics for scientific instruments sits at the intersection of three rapidly evolving fields: open-source scientific hardware practice, modular motor/sensor electronics engineering, and emerging generative and automated electronic design automation (EDA). This review synthesizes 14 key references (2018–2025) spanning these domains, with explicit relevance to the design of manufacturable, easily replicated control PCBs for a multi-channel powder dosing device. The following sections are organized around the three requested topic areas, followed by a gap analysis of generative/automated EDA maturity.

### 2. Open-Hardware Electronics Design Practice and Reproducibility (Topic a)

The open scientific hardware community has developed a coherent set of design norms over the past decade, codified by projects, community roadmaps, and dedicated journals.

**Poseidon syringe pump system.** Booeshaghi et al. (2019) articulated seven design principles for open bioinstrumentation—functionality, robustness, safety, simplicity, modularity, benchmarking, and documentation—using the Poseidon open-source syringe pump as a worked example. The system uses an Arduino Uno with a CNC shield driving up to four stepper-motor pumps, paired with a Raspberry Pi and touchscreen for microscope control. The total cost is under $400 and assembly takes less than one hour. The authors emphasize that adoption depends on precise BOMs with vendor links, build videos, cross-platform executables, and firmware loaded via Arduino IDE (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000007). Critically, the electronics architecture relies entirely on off-the-shelf boards rather than custom PCBs, using configurable microstepping jumpers on the CNC shield to set flow-rate ranges (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000041).

**HardOps for OpenFlexure.** Stirling et al. (2022) introduced HardOps, adapting software DevOps workflows—distributed version control, CI pipelines, issue tracking, and linked multi-repository management—to hardware design. The OpenFlexure microscope project maintains separate repositories for mechanical hardware, software, electronics, embedded OS, project website, and failure mode analysis, all integrated through GitLab CI pipelines that can automatically generate production files and track feedback from distributed manufacturing and testing (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024). This approach is significant because it addresses process infrastructure rather than just device design, enabling reproducible manufacturing across multiple sites and countries.

**GOSH Roadmap.** The Global Open Science Hardware community roadmap (2018) defines open science hardware as hardware that can be "obtained, assembled, used, studied, modified, shared, and sold by anyone" and recommends recognized licenses (CERN OHL, TAPR OHL, Solderpad), documentation standards, contextual quality metrics, and support structures including shared fabrication facilities (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000042, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000046). The roadmap is governance-oriented rather than technical, but its norms directly shape how control PCBs should be documented and released.

**Open microscopy norms.** Hohlbein et al. (2022) synthesized best practices in a Nature Methods comment, stressing that open hardware should publish editable source files (not just STL or PDF exports), provide calibration references, and build plugin-oriented software interfaces for long-term device interoperability. They also emphasized that distributing hardware is fundamentally harder than distributing software because of non-zero replication cost and assembly complexity (pqac-00000005).

**Jubilee and Science-Jubilee.** Subbaraman et al. (2024) demonstrated the Duckbot, a plant biology automation system built on the Jubilee open-source tool-changing motion platform. Jubilee uses Duet3 controller boards running RepRap firmware, with a Raspberry Pi 4 for higher-level computation and notebook-driven experiment control. The system supports interchangeable 3D-printed and laser-cut tools (pipettes, cameras, syringes) attached via a tool-changing mechanism, with the Science-Jubilee Python package wrapping G-code control and exposing OpenTrons-compatible labware definitions (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013). The base kit costs approximately $1,700 and can be assembled from off-the-shelf components.

**From DIY to global transformation.** Wenzel (2023) situated open hardware as a transition from ad hoc maker projects to globally distributed laboratory technology, emphasizing that reducing dependence on import logistics and proprietary supply chains is as important as headline cost reduction. Central drivers of adoption include open sharing, digital fabrication, standard parts, local production, and detailed documentation (pqac-00000031).

**Lessons from Wikimedia fellowships.** Keller et al. (2023) reflected on practical open-hardware development, noting recurring challenges in transitioning from "flying cable" prototypes to reproducible PCB-based builds. They described trade-offs between custom controller boards and reliance on poorly documented commodity modules, and stressed the need for early repository discipline, explicit module boundaries, and realistic attention to sourcing and proprietary dependencies (pqac-00000003).

### 3. Modular and Expandable Motor/Sensor Control Electronics (Topic b)

**Modular multi-axis stepper driver.** Fischer et al. (2025) described a low-cost (~$360) open-source multi-axis stepper controller for microscopy, built around a Raspberry Pi Pico 2 microcontroller and up to four Trinamic TMC5240 evaluation boards communicating via SPI. The system supports per-axis encoders and limit switches via DB-9 connectors, implements open-loop, open-loop with pull-in correction, and closed-loop control modes, and includes a separate remote controller with joystick and rotary encoder inputs linked via UART over RJ-45. Software includes C and Python libraries and a µManager driver. The authors explicitly note that they chose evaluation boards over custom PCBs to simplify construction, but propose future PCB integration to reduce soldering and cost (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021). This staged approach—module-based prototype first, custom PCB second—is a recurring pattern in open scientific hardware.

**Open-source lab-grade scales.** Hubbard and Pearce (2020) provided a thoroughly documented open-source digital scale using an Arduino Nano, HX711 load-cell amplifier, and 3D-printed enclosure. The design achieves 0.05 g repeatability (0.005 g with small-range load cells), supports serial logging compliant with the SMA SCP-0499 protocol, and can operate standalone with LCD or as a USB-connected logging instrument. Component placement uses entirely 3D-printed snap-fit features, minimizing fasteners (pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031). This is directly relevant to gravimetric feedback in multi-channel powder dosing.

**Pellet dispensomixer.** Hernández-del-Valle et al. (2024) presented the closest published analog to a multi-material powder dosing system: an eight-channel pellet dispensomixer using Arduino MEGA-controlled stepper motors with A4988 drivers, an external balance for mass-flow feedback, and iteratively designed 3D-printed rotors with anti-clogging features. The system costs approximately €250 and is intended for automated nanocomposite compounding and self-driving laboratory workflows. Control code communicates via serial with a host computer running Python notebooks that orchestrate dispensing and balance feedback (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027).

**Open-source SECM.** Guver et al. (2019) demonstrated a scanning electrochemical microscope built under $300 from an open-source DStat potentiostat, Arduino-controlled stepper motors with driver shields, GRBL motion control software, and 3D-printed mechanics. The project exemplifies HardwareX publication norms of cost transparency, open source files, and clear buildability instructions (pqac-00000040).

### 4. Generative and Automated EDA for Scientific Instruments (Topic c)

**ML for EDA survey.** Huang et al. (2021) provided a comprehensive pre-LLM survey of machine learning applications in electronic design automation, covering estimation, optimization, verification, and other tasks across the IC design hierarchy. Critically, the entire survey focuses on VLSI/integrated-circuit flows; board-level PCB design for scientific instruments is not addressed (pqac-00000032).

**Large Circuit Models.** Chen et al. (2024) proposed AI-native EDA built around Large Circuit Models (LCMs)—multimodal foundation models that would jointly represent specification, RTL, netlist, floorplan, and layout information. While ambitious, the proposal is entirely framed around integrated-circuit design abstractions. There is no analogous effort for board-level scientific instrument PCBs (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000035).

**Generative AI for analog IC design.** Zadeh and Elamien (2025) surveyed the application of GNNs, VAEs, and LLMs to analog circuit sizing and topology exploration, highlighting tools such as AnalogCoder and AmpAgent. These approaches remain focused on IC-level design and do not address board-level instrument electronics (pqac-00000037).

**SmartonAI for KiCad.** Han et al. (2023/2025) developed SmartonAI, an LLM-assisted interaction plugin for KiCad that uses retrieval-augmented generation and task decomposition to help users navigate documentation, invoke plugins, and execute design operations through natural language. This is the only published work directly touching open-source PCB tooling with AI assistance. However, SmartonAI assists with tool interaction rather than generating circuit designs: it lowers the skill barrier to using KiCad but does not solve component selection, circuit topology, EMI-aware placement, or manufacturable routing (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000033).

### 5. Gap Analysis: How Mature Is Generative/Automated Electrical Design for Open Scientific Hardware?

The evidence reveals a stark gap between the ambitions of generative EDA research and the practical needs of open scientific hardware electronics designers. The key findings are:

1. **All published ML/AI EDA work targets VLSI/IC flows.** The surveys by Huang et al. (2021), Chen et al. (2024), Zadeh and Elamien (2025), and Xu et al. Board-level PCB design for laboratory instruments is entirely absent from this literature (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000037, pqac-00000039).

2. **SmartonAI is the closest tool to open-hardware PCB automation, but it is an assistant, not a generator.** It helps users interact with KiCad through natural language but does not create schematics, select components, or produce layouts (pqac-00000033).

3. **Open-hardware projects avoid custom PCB design by using off-the-shelf modules.** Poseidon uses Arduino + CNC shield (pqac-00000001); Fischer et al. use TMC evaluation boards (pqac-00000021); Hernández-del-Valle et al. use Arduino MEGA + A4988 driver modules (pqac-00000025); Hubbard and Pearce use Arduino Nano + HX711 breakout (pqac-00000030).
